class_schedule.topic: The Marketing Concept and Customer Value Creation
learning_objectives: Define marketing as a value-creation discipline rather than a sales or advertising function and articulate the AMA's current definition
Distinguish among the production, product, selling, marketing, and societal-marketing orientations and identify which orientation a real company practices
Decompose customer value into the perceived-benefits-minus-perceived-costs equation and explain why both sides must be measured
Explain the relationship among customer value, customer satisfaction, customer retention, and customer lifetime value as the chain that produces sustained profit
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 1 - Defining Marketing for the New Realities
Armstrong & Kotler, Marketing: An Introduction (15th ed.), Ch. 1 - Marketing: Creating Customer Value and Engagement
Theodore Levitt, 'Marketing Myopia,' Harvard Business Review (1960, reprinted 2004) - foundational article on customer orientation
Peter Drucker, The Practice of Management (1954), Ch. 5 - 'The Purpose of a Business is to Create a Customer'

lecture_notes.introduction: Most people think marketing is advertising. They are wrong. Advertising is one tactic inside one of the four Ps inside the marketing function, and a company that confuses the two will overspend on ads while failing at the actual job of marketing, which is to identify a group of people whose needs are not adequately served, design something they will value more than the alternatives, make it available where they shop, price it at a level that captures fair value for the firm, and communicate its existence in a way that earns their trust. Peter Drucker argued in 1954 that the purpose of a business is not to make a profit but to create a customer, and that profit is the reward for doing this well. Theodore Levitt extended the argument in 1960 with 'Marketing Myopia,' showing that the railroads went bankrupt not because of declining demand for transportation but because they defined themselves as railroads instead of transportation providers and missed the rise of trucking, automobiles, and aviation. The same myopia killed Kodak (which invented the digital camera in 1975 but defined itself as a film company), Blockbuster (which defined itself as a store rather than a movie-on-demand service), and Nokia (which defined itself as a phone hardware maker rather than a mobile-software platform). This course teaches you to think like a marketer in the strategic sense: not as a writer of clever ads but as a manager who decides which customers to serve, which value to deliver, and how to capture value in return.
key_concepts: American Marketing Association definition (current): 'Marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large' - notice that exchange and value are central, advertising is not mentioned

Customer perceived value = perceived total benefits (functional, emotional, social, experiential) minus perceived total costs (monetary, time, energy, psychological); customers do not buy products, they buy bundles of benefits at a perceived cost

Five marketing orientations evolution: production (cheaper and more available wins, e.g., early Ford Model T), product (best features win, the engineering trap), selling (push hard enough and customers will buy, e.g., door-to-door encyclopedias), marketing (understand needs first then build, e.g., Procter & Gamble), societal marketing (customer needs plus societal welfare, e.g., Patagonia)

Marketing myopia (Levitt 1960): defining your business by the product you make rather than the customer need you serve; railroads defined themselves as railroad companies instead of transportation companies and lost to airlines and trucking
The marketing concept: organizational success comes from being more effective than competitors at creating, delivering, and communicating customer value to a chosen target market

Customer satisfaction = perceived performance compared to expectations; satisfaction is not absolute, it is relative to what the customer expected, which is why over-promising in advertising can kill an otherwise good product

Customer lifetime value (CLV): the total profit a customer generates across their entire relationship with the firm, discounted to present value; the foundation of all rational customer-acquisition spending

The retention-profit chain: small improvements in retention compound dramatically; Frederick Reichheld's research at Bain shows that a 5 percent improvement in customer retention can lift profits 25-95 percent depending on the industry

Holistic marketing concept (Kotler): integration of internal marketing, integrated marketing, relationship marketing, and performance marketing into a unified approach rather than treating marketing as a single department's siloed responsibility

Needs vs wants vs demands: a need is a basic human requirement (food, safety, belonging), a want is the specific way that need is shaped by culture and personality (a Big Mac or a salad), a demand is a want backed by ability to pay; marketers do not create needs, they shape wants and stimulate demand
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case walkthrough comparing three coffee businesses to make the value-equation concrete. Take a McDonald's coffee at $1.99 versus a Starbucks latte at $5.45 versus a Blue Bottle pour-over at $7.50. Decompose each on the value equation: McDonald's delivers low monetary cost, fast service, predictable taste, and convenient drive-through (functional benefits dominate). Starbucks delivers a third place between home and work, customizable drinks, loyalty rewards, mobile ordering, and social signaling (experiential and social benefits dominate). Blue Bottle delivers single-origin sourcing, barista craft, and connoisseur identity (experiential benefits with high psychological cost of waiting). Each succeeds with a different segment because each defines value differently. Then ask: which firm is most vulnerable to a recession and why? Answer: Blue Bottle, because the experiential and identity benefits are most discretionary, while McDonald's coffee is recession-resistant.
setup_requirements: Kotler & Keller textbook (16th ed.) physical or digital copy
HBR archive access for the Levitt 'Marketing Myopia' article
Spreadsheet (Excel or Google Sheets) for in-class CLV calculation exercise
Whiteboard or Miro for value-equation decomposition exercise

review.value: Marketing is the strategic discipline of choosing which customers to serve, what value to deliver, and how to capture value back; advertising is one promotion tactic. A company can have excellent advertising and terrible marketing if it is selling the wrong product to the wrong segment at the wrong price

review.key: Value Is in the Customer's Head
review.value: Value is not what the firm thinks it built, it is what the customer perceives. Two products with identical features can deliver completely different value because perception is shaped by brand, context, and prior experience. This is why Apple charges 3x the BOM cost of an iPhone while commodity Android makers compete on price

Always define your business by the customer need you serve, not the product you currently sell. Netflix defined itself as 'in-home entertainment' and survived the DVD-to-streaming transition; Blockbuster defined itself as 'video rental stores' and died. The right definition determines which threats and opportunities you can see

Acquiring a new customer costs 5-25x as much as retaining an existing one (Bain & Company research), and existing customers buy more, recommend more, and are less price-sensitive. A marketing strategy that ignores retention in favor of pure acquisition is leaking the bucket faster than it can be filled

class_schedule.topic: Marketing Environment and PESTEL Analysis for Marketers
learning_objectives: Differentiate the firm's microenvironment (suppliers, intermediaries, customers, competitors, publics) from its macroenvironment (PESTEL forces)
Conduct a PESTEL analysis of a specific industry and identify which forces are accelerating, decelerating, or stable
Evaluate how environmental scanning informs strategic marketing choices like which segments to enter and which to abandon
Apply scenario planning to anticipate how environmental shifts might reshape a firm's competitive position over the next 3-5 years
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 3 - Collecting Information and Forecasting Demand
Armstrong & Kotler, Marketing: An Introduction (15th ed.), Ch. 3 - Analyzing the Marketing Environment
Francis Aguilar, Scanning the Business Environment (1967) - origin of PEST framework
Harvard Business Review, 'Living in the Futures' (Kees van der Heijden, 2013) - on scenario planning at Royal Dutch Shell

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing decisions made without scanning the environment are like driving with the windshield painted black. The forces that shape whether your strategy works are mostly outside your firm and outside your control: demographic shifts that grow or shrink your target segment, economic cycles that change willingness to pay, technological breakthroughs that obsolete your product category, regulatory changes that ban your business model, social movements that make your brand suddenly tone-deaf. Kodak built a billion-dollar film business unaware that digital photography would render it obsolete in 15 years even though Kodak's own engineers had invented the digital camera in 1975. Blockbuster's executives knew streaming was coming but assumed they had years to react. The Quaker Oats acquisition of Snapple in 1994 collapsed because the company misread the cultural environment that made Snapple authentic and tried to mass-merchandise it. Environmental scanning is not optional, and the PESTEL framework (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal) is the standard tool for it. This session teaches you how to look at your industry through six lenses systematically, separate signal from noise, and convert environmental insight into strategic action. By the end you will be able to walk into a strategy meeting and say with evidence, 'Here are the three external forces that will reshape this market in the next five years, and here is what we should do about each one.'
key_concepts: Microenvironment: actors close to the firm that affect its ability to serve customers - suppliers, marketing intermediaries (resellers, logistics, agencies, financial intermediaries), customer markets (consumer, business, reseller, government, international), competitors, and publics (financial, media, government, citizen-action, local, general, internal)
Macroenvironment: broader societal forces that affect the entire microenvironment, captured by the PESTEL framework

Political forces: government stability, trade policy, taxation, antitrust posture, subsidies; e.g., the EU's Digital Markets Act forcing Apple to allow third-party app stores has reshaped the iOS marketing landscape

Economic forces: GDP growth, interest rates, inflation, unemployment, consumer confidence, exchange rates, income distribution; e.g., the 2022-2024 inflation cycle pushed consumers toward private label and discount channels like Aldi and Costco at the expense of premium brands

Social forces: demographics (age, generational cohorts, household composition, immigration, urbanization), values, lifestyles, education levels; e.g., Millennials and Gen Z drove the rise of plant-based foods and sustainability-focused brands like Beyond Meat and Allbirds

Technological forces: innovation pace, R&D spending, automation, platform shifts, AI capabilities; e.g., the rise of large language models in 2022-2024 reshaped search marketing as users began asking ChatGPT instead of Google

Environmental forces: climate change, resource scarcity, pollution regulation, sustainability expectations, ESG investor pressure; e.g., Patagonia's Worn Wear program turns environmental consciousness into a marketing advantage

Legal forces: consumer protection laws, advertising regulation, privacy law (GDPR, CCPA), labor law, IP law; e.g., Apple's App Tracking Transparency in iOS 14.5 cost Meta an estimated $10 billion in 2022 revenue by limiting cross-app tracking

Demographic shifts as the most predictable macroforce: birth rates, aging populations, household formation, and immigration patterns are knowable years in advance; brands that ignore them (e.g., toy makers ignoring the falling birth rate) are blindsided

Scenario planning: building 2-4 internally consistent stories of how the future might unfold (rather than a single forecast), then stress-testing strategy against each one; pioneered by Pierre Wack at Royal Dutch Shell in the 1970s, which is how Shell anticipated the 1973 oil crisis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live PESTEL exercise on the U.S. automotive industry. Political: EV tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act, tariffs on Chinese EV imports, CAFE fuel economy standards. Economic: high interest rates raising auto loan payments and depressing demand, used car price normalization after 2021-2022 spike. Social: Gen Z showing lower interest in car ownership, growth of urban living and ride-sharing, persistent SUV preference despite environmental concerns. Technological: battery cost curves, autonomous driving progress, software-defined vehicles, OTA updates. Environmental: ICE phase-out commitments in California and EU, lithium and nickel supply constraints, end-of-life battery recycling. Legal: NHTSA crash standards for autonomous vehicles, dealer franchise laws blocking Tesla's direct sales in some states, right-to-repair legislation. Synthesize: Tesla benefits from technological and environmental tailwinds but faces political risk from changing tax credits and dealer-franchise legal pressure. Ford and GM face the most disruptive transition because they must wind down profitable ICE businesses while losing money on EVs.
setup_requirements: PESTEL template handout with prompting questions for each category
Access to Statista, IBISWorld, or similar industry research database
Recent Federal Reserve economic indicators dashboard (FRED.stlouisfed.org)
Industry-specific trade publication (Automotive News, Modern Retail, etc.) for the chosen industry

review.value: PESTEL is not a one-time exercise for the annual planning offsite; it should be a continuous process with assigned owners watching each force. Companies that scan continuously catch shifts when they are small and cheap to react to; companies that scan annually wake up in a crisis

Demographic forces are the most predictable and longest-lasting; the number of 18-year-olds in the U.S. in 2030 is already known because they are alive today. Brands that align with demographic trends (aging population, household downsizing, urbanization) ride durable tailwinds

Environmental forces usually affect every player in your industry, so the question is not 'is this bad for us' but 'is this worse for us than for our competitors.' Asymmetric impact is what creates winners and losers; symmetric impact just shifts the whole industry

When the future is genuinely uncertain (will AI commoditize content marketing? will privacy law kill behavioral targeting?), build 2-4 distinct scenarios and ensure your strategy works acceptably in each one. Avoid betting the company on a single forecast you cannot defend

class_schedule.topic: Consumer Behavior: Decision Process and Psychological Influences
learning_objectives: Map the five-stage consumer decision process (need recognition, information search, evaluation of alternatives, purchase, post-purchase) and identify where marketing can intervene at each stage
Distinguish among cultural, social, personal, and psychological influences on consumer behavior using real product examples
Apply theories from behavioral economics (loss aversion, anchoring, social proof, default bias) to explain real consumer choices
Analyze post-purchase dissonance and identify marketing tactics that reduce it
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 6 - Analyzing Consumer Markets
Solomon, Consumer Behavior: Buying, Having, and Being (13th ed.), Ch. 1-3 and Ch. 8
Daniel Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011), Ch. 26-28 on prospect theory and loss aversion
Robert Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (revised ed.), Ch. 4 on social proof and Ch. 6 on scarcity

lecture_notes.introduction: Customers are not rational utility maximizers running spreadsheets in their heads. They are messy bundles of habit, emotion, social pressure, mental shortcuts, and post-hoc rationalization. A marketer who designs for the rational customer in the textbook will fail when meeting the actual human in the store. Daniel Kahneman won the Nobel Prize in economics for showing that people use System 1 (fast, automatic, emotional) for most decisions and only use System 2 (slow, deliberate, analytical) when forced to. Most purchases are System 1: brand-driven, habitual, anchored to familiar prices, biased toward defaults, vulnerable to social proof. Robert Cialdini catalogued the seven universal influence principles that exploit System 1: reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, liking, authority, scarcity, and unity. Brands that understand these principles design experiences that work with human cognition, not against it. Amazon's one-click purchase removes cognitive friction at the moment of decision; restaurant menus highlight a 'most popular' dish to invoke social proof; airlines use scarcity messaging ('only 3 seats left at this price') to trigger loss aversion; subscription services use the default-bias cancellation friction to retain customers who would otherwise leave. This session teaches you how real consumers actually decide, why they choose the things they choose, and how to design marketing that works with the grain of human psychology rather than against it.
key_concepts: Five-stage decision process (Engel-Kollat-Blackwell): need recognition (problem awareness), information search (internal memory and external sources), evaluation of alternatives (consideration set, evaluative criteria), purchase decision (often non-rational), post-purchase behavior (satisfaction, dissonance, repeat intent)

Need recognition triggers: internal stimuli (hunger, thirst, social need) and external stimuli (advertising, product display, competitor's product, life event); marketers can activate need recognition through advertising or seasonal/event-based prompts

Consideration set vs awareness set vs choice set: customers know about hundreds of brands but seriously consider only 3-5 in any category; getting into the consideration set is the critical battle that branding wins or loses

Cultural influences: culture (broadest values), subculture (nationality, religion, ethnicity, geography), social class; e.g., the rise of K-pop and K-beauty shows how cultural exports reshape global consumer preferences
Social influences: reference groups (membership, aspirational, dissociative), opinion leaders, family roles, status; influencer marketing exploits aspirational reference groups

Personal influences: age and life-cycle stage, occupation, economic situation, lifestyle (psychographics), personality and self-concept; the same person makes different decisions at age 25, 35, and 55

Psychological influences: motivation (Maslow's hierarchy, Freudian latent motives, Herzberg's two-factor), perception (selective attention, distortion, retention), learning (drive, cues, response, reinforcement), beliefs and attitudes

Behavioral economics insights: loss aversion (losses hurt 2-2.5x more than equivalent gains feel good), anchoring (first number seen biases all subsequent estimates), decoy effect (adding a third inferior option shifts choice between two), default bias (people stick with whatever option is preselected), choice overload (too many options reduce decision likelihood)

Cognitive dissonance (Festinger): the discomfort of holding two contradictory beliefs, especially after major purchases; marketers reduce post-purchase dissonance through follow-up communications, return guarantees, and customer-success outreach

Habit and the cue-routine-reward loop (Charles Duhigg): most repeat purchases are habits triggered by cues, not deliberate choices; brands that own a cue (Pepsodent and the 'film on your teeth' cue invented by Claude Hopkins) own the habit
lecture_notes.demonstration: Behavioral economics in restaurant menu design. Walk through how a casual dining chain redesigned its menu using behavioral principles. Anchoring: place a $48 ribeye in the upper-right corner where eyes land first; the actual best-selling item is a $24 burger, which now feels reasonable next to the anchor. Decoy effect: offer 8 oz steak at $26, 12 oz steak at $32, and 16 oz steak at $34; the 16 oz makes the 12 oz feel like the rational middle choice and shifts mix toward the higher-margin item. Social proof: badge three items as 'guest favorites' to reduce decision anxiety. Default bias: include a side salad and bread service in entree price by default; opting out feels like losing something. Scarcity: 'Featured this week only' label on a high-margin special. Calculate the financial impact: if the redesigned menu shifts mix by 3 percent toward higher-margin items on $5M annual revenue, that is $150K of incremental contribution margin with zero cost increase.
setup_requirements: Sample restaurant menus (high-end and casual) for redesign exercise
Cialdini's Influence book or summary handout
Behavioral economics field guide (Behavioral Insights Team's EAST framework PDF)
Whiteboard for mapping decision-process intervention points

review.value: If your brand is not among the 3-5 brands a customer mentally considers when entering the category, you are not even competing for the sale. Branding's primary job is to ensure your name comes to mind in the relevant category. This is why category-entry-point marketing (Romaniuk and Sharp) emphasizes mental availability over persuasion

Most decisions are made fast, emotional, and automatic. Packaging, color, brand familiarity, social proof, and price anchors do more than feature lists. A product page that requires 10 minutes of careful comparison will lose to one that lets the customer decide in 30 seconds and feel good about it

After major purchases, customers second-guess themselves. Post-purchase emails that reinforce the decision ('Welcome to the family! Here is what to look forward to'), generous return policies, and customer-success outreach all reduce buyer's remorse and convert one-time buyers into repeat customers and advocates

Persuading a customer to switch is hard; making your brand the default through habit formation is the path to durable share. Subscription models, auto-replenishment, and loyalty programs all work because they convert deliberate choices into automatic habits

learning_objectives: Identify the differences between consumer and organizational buying processes in terms of buying center composition, decision criteria, and sales cycle length
Map the eight stages of the organizational buying process and identify marketing-relevant intervention points
Distinguish among new task, modified rebuy, and straight rebuy purchase situations and select appropriate marketing tactics for each
Apply buying center analysis to identify the users, influencers, deciders, approvers, buyers, and gatekeepers in a real B2B sale
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 7 - Analyzing Business Markets
Hutt & Speh, Business Marketing Management: B2B (12th ed.), Ch. 2-3
Webster & Wind, 'A General Model for Understanding Organizational Buying Behavior' (1972) - foundational framework
Brent Adamson, Matthew Dixon, Pat Spenner, 'The New Sales Imperative,' Harvard Business Review (2017)

lecture_notes.introduction: Selling a $50 pair of sneakers to a teenager and selling a $5 million ERP implementation to a Fortune 500 manufacturer are utterly different problems requiring different marketing playbooks. B2B buying has more decision-makers, more steps, longer cycles, more rational evaluation criteria, and higher stakes. The Webster and Wind framework from 1972 still organizes how we think about it: a buying center includes users (who will use the product), influencers (technical experts who shape specs), deciders (who choose vendors), approvers (who authorize), buyers (who handle procurement), and gatekeepers (who control information flow). Marketing that targets only the buyer fails because the buyer often does not have decision authority; marketing that targets only the decider fails because the influencers can veto the choice. Modern B2B buying has gotten harder, not easier. CEB research found that the average B2B purchase decision now involves 6.8 stakeholders, up from 5.4 a few years prior, and that 'group consensus' is now the single biggest reason deals stall. Add to this the rise of self-service buying (Gartner found that 77 percent of B2B buyers describe their last purchase as complex or difficult), and the role of B2B marketing has shifted from generating leads for sales reps to helping buying committees build consensus.
key_concepts: Key differences between B2B and B2C: fewer but larger buyers, derived demand (B2B demand depends on consumer demand downstream), more inelastic short-run demand, more fluctuating demand (acceleration principle), professional purchasing, multiple buying influences, multiple sales calls over longer cycles, direct purchasing more common

Buying center roles (Webster & Wind, 1972): users (who use the product), influencers (technical staff who shape requirements), deciders (formal authority for vendor selection), approvers (sign-off on the deal), buyers (procurement, contract negotiation), gatekeepers (control information access, e.g., executive assistants)

Three buying situations: straight rebuy (routine reorder, supplier selection rarely revisited, vendors must defend incumbent position), modified rebuy (some terms changing, opening for new vendors), new task (first-time purchase with significant uncertainty, longest cycle and most stakeholders involved)

Eight-stage organizational buying process (Robinson, Faris, Wind): problem recognition, general need description, product specification, supplier search, proposal solicitation, supplier selection, order-routine specification, performance review

Derived demand: B2B demand is derived from consumer demand; if consumer demand for cars falls, demand for steel, tires, and semiconductors falls in proportion. B2B marketers must monitor downstream consumer trends

Acceleration principle: small changes in consumer demand cause large changes in B2B demand because buyers adjust inventory levels in addition to consumption; a 10 percent drop in retail sales can cause a 30 percent drop in factory orders
Vendor evaluation criteria in B2B: product quality, price, delivery reliability, technical support, vendor reputation, total cost of ownership, integration capability, financial stability of supplier

Total cost of ownership (TCO): the full economic cost of a purchase including acquisition, training, integration, maintenance, downtime, and end-of-life disposal; B2B marketers shift the conversation from price to TCO to justify premium pricing
Account-based marketing (ABM): B2B strategy that treats individual target accounts as markets of one, with personalized content, targeted ads, and coordinated outreach across the buying center

Consensus selling (CEB research): the fact that B2B deals stall most often because the buying committee cannot agree internally; sales and marketing must arm internal champions with content that helps them convince colleagues
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map the buying center for a hospital system purchasing a $2 million surgical robot. Users: surgeons who will operate the robot. Influencers: hospital department chairs, biomedical engineers, nursing leadership. Deciders: surgical services VP plus chief medical officer. Approvers: CFO (capital budget), CEO (strategic priority), board capital committee (above $1M threshold). Buyers: procurement team. Gatekeepers: executive assistants who control calendar access, plus IT security team that must approve any networked medical device. Then map the marketing intervention plan: peer-reviewed clinical evidence for surgeons, TCO analysis for CFO, case studies from similar hospitals for the CMO, security and compliance documentation for IT, and ROI calculator for the procurement team. A vendor that pitches only the surgeon's clinical excitement and ignores the CFO's TCO concerns will lose to a vendor that arms every stakeholder with the content they need.
setup_requirements: B2B buying center mapping template
Sample RFP document for educational reference
Case study reading: Salesforce ABM playbook or Demandbase resources
TCO calculator template (Excel or Google Sheets)

review.key: Sell to the Whole Buying Center
review.value: B2B deals are won and lost across multiple stakeholders, not single decision-makers. A marketing program that produces beautiful content for the user but nothing for the CFO will close deals slowly or not at all. Map the buying center for every target account and ensure each role has content that addresses their specific concern

When competing on price alone, the cheapest vendor wins. When competing on total cost of ownership including downtime, integration cost, training, and lifecycle, the highest-quality vendor often wins even at 2-3x the upfront price. B2B marketers must teach buyers to think in TCO terms

Even when one person inside the buying organization loves your product, they have to convince the rest of the committee. Provide internal-selling tools (one-pagers, ROI calculators, case studies, FAQ documents) that the champion can forward to colleagues. This is the most common reason deals close late or stall entirely

New-task purchases require deep education and risk reduction. Straight rebuys require relationship maintenance and incumbency defense. Modified rebuys are the opening for new vendors to displace incumbents. Apply the wrong playbook and you waste resources

class_schedule.topic: Marketing Research Methods and Survey Design
learning_objectives: Distinguish exploratory, descriptive, and causal research designs and select the appropriate design for a given marketing question
Differentiate primary from secondary data sources and quantitative from qualitative methods, with criteria for choosing among them
Design a survey instrument that avoids common biases (leading questions, double-barreled, social desirability, satisficing)
Apply ethical principles to marketing research including informed consent, privacy, and honest reporting of findings
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 4 - Conducting Marketing Research
Malhotra, Marketing Research: An Applied Orientation (7th ed.), Ch. 3-5
Naresh Malhotra and David Birks, Marketing Research: An Applied Approach (5th ed.), Ch. 6
Harvard Business Review, 'Marketing Malpractice: The Cause and the Cure' (Christensen, Cook & Hall, 2005) on Jobs-to-be-Done research

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing decisions made without evidence are gambling. Marketing research is the systematic collection and analysis of data to reduce uncertainty in marketing decisions, and it spans methods from quick polls to multi-million-dollar product concept tests. The classic disaster is New Coke in 1985: Coca-Cola spent millions testing the new formula in blind taste tests against Pepsi and old Coke, and the new formula won. They launched it, and consumers revolted, forcing the return of Coca-Cola Classic within 79 days. The research was technically correct but missed the question that mattered: would loyal Coke drinkers accept the removal of the original formula? The taste test measured taste preference; the actual decision required measuring identity and emotional attachment. This is the central truth of marketing research: the method must match the question. Surveys are great for measuring known preferences across large samples but bad for discovering unknown unknowns. Focus groups are great for surfacing language and emotional reactions but terrible for forecasting market share. Conjoint analysis is great for measuring trade-offs in feature bundles but useless if you have not first identified the relevant features. This session teaches you to choose the right research design, recognize the failure modes of each method, design instruments that produce valid data, and report findings honestly even when they contradict the strategy you wanted to validate.
key_concepts: Research design types: exploratory (loose, qualitative, used to discover and define problems, e.g., focus groups, depth interviews), descriptive (structured, quantitative, used to describe market characteristics, e.g., surveys), causal (experimental, used to test cause-and-effect, e.g., A/B tests, conjoint)

Primary vs secondary data: secondary is existing data (industry reports, government statistics, syndicated panels like Nielsen and IRI, internal sales data), primary is data collected for the specific problem at hand; always exhaust secondary first because it is faster and cheaper

Qualitative methods: focus groups (8-10 participants, 90 minutes, moderator-led discussion), depth interviews (one-on-one, 60-90 minutes, exploration of latent motives), ethnography (observing customers in their natural environment), netnography (analyzing online communities), projective techniques (sentence completion, word association)
Quantitative methods: surveys (online, phone, mail, intercept), observation, experiments (lab and field), conjoint analysis, choice modeling, scanner data analysis, panel data

Sampling: probability samples (simple random, stratified, cluster) allow statistical inference to population; non-probability samples (convenience, judgmental, quota, snowball) are faster and cheaper but cannot be statistically generalized

Sample size considerations: for proportions at 95 percent confidence and 5 percent margin of error, n is approximately 384 regardless of population size for large populations; for sub-group analysis, multiply by number of sub-groups

Survey question quality: avoid leading questions ('Don't you agree that our product is excellent?'), double-barreled ('Is this product fast and reliable?' is two questions), loaded language, ambiguous wording, and questions that exceed the respondent's knowledge

Common response biases: acquiescence bias (tendency to agree), social desirability bias (giving the answer the respondent thinks is acceptable), satisficing (giving the first acceptable answer to finish quickly), order effects (earlier questions affecting later answers)

Jobs-to-be-Done (Christensen): instead of asking customers what they want, observe what jobs they are hiring products to do; the classic milkshake study found that morning milkshakes were 'hired' for the job of 'making my long commute less boring while keeping me full until lunch,' which led to product improvements like making the milkshake thicker so it lasted longer through the straw

Research ethics: informed consent (participants know what they are participating in), privacy and anonymity, honest data collection (no fabricating or selectively reporting), ESOMAR and Insights Association codes of conduct
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a research plan for a hypothetical client: a meal-kit company (think HelloFresh or Blue Apron) considering a new product line of breakfast kits. Phase 1 - Exploratory (4 weeks): 12 depth interviews with current subscribers and 12 with churned subscribers to understand morning eating habits and reactions to the concept. Phase 2 - Descriptive (3 weeks): online survey of 800 subscribers measuring interest, willingness to pay at three price points, and preferred meal types. Phase 3 - Causal (6 weeks): conjoint analysis of 600 respondents to measure trade-offs among 5 key attributes (price, prep time, ingredient quality, meal variety, dietary options); plus a real product test where 200 households receive sample kits and report on actual usage. Total cost: approximately $120,000. Compare this to launching nationally without research and discovering after $5M in marketing that demand is lower than expected; the research is cheap insurance.
setup_requirements: Sample survey instruments showing good and bad question design
Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey, or Google Forms account for in-class survey design exercise
Sample syndicated research report (Nielsen, Mintel, Statista) for secondary data exercise
Conjoint analysis demo (Sawtooth Software or Conjoint.ly tutorial)

review.value: There is no universally best research method; each method answers some questions and misses others. Surveys cannot reveal unknown needs; focus groups cannot forecast share; A/B tests cannot test concepts that do not yet exist. Choose the method that fits the decision you need to inform

It is tempting to design research that produces the answer you already want and to ignore findings that contradict your hypothesis. The New Coke disaster happened in part because Coca-Cola's executives wanted to find a winning formula and stopped asking whether removing the original formula was acceptable. Test your assumptions, not your conclusions

What people say they will do and what they actually do diverge wildly. 70 percent of survey respondents say they would buy a green product, but only 15-25 percent actually do at the point of purchase. Observed behavior (purchase data, A/B tests, ethnography) is far more reliable than stated intent

Spending $100K on research that prevents a $10M launch failure is the best ROI in marketing. The cost of research feels real and immediate; the cost of failed launches is much larger but spread out and easy to rationalize. Always quantify the cost of being wrong before deciding the research budget

learning_objectives: Apply the four bases of consumer segmentation (geographic, demographic, psychographic, behavioral) and the most useful B2B segmentation bases
Evaluate segments using the criteria of measurability, accessibility, substantiality, differentiability, and actionability
Choose among undifferentiated, differentiated, concentrated, and micro-marketing targeting strategies given a firm's resources and competitive position
Construct a positioning statement that names the target, the frame of reference, the point of difference, and the reason to believe
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 9 - Identifying Market Segments and Targets
Al Ries and Jack Trout, Positioning: The Battle for Your Mind (20th anniv. ed.) - foundational positioning text
Byron Sharp, How Brands Grow: What Marketers Don't Know (2010), Ch. 2-4 on the limits of differentiation
Harvard Business Review, 'Why the Most Successful Marketers Use Personas' (Bonchek & France, 2014)

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot serve everyone. Every successful brand has at its core a chosen group of customers it serves better than anyone else, and a chosen group of customers it deliberately ignores. Trying to serve everyone produces a brand that is mediocre at everything and beloved by no one. Walmart serves price-conscious shoppers; trying to add high-end fashion to its stores would alienate its core customer without convincing the high-end shopper. Tesla serves environmentally conscious affluent buyers; trying to add a $20,000 economy car would dilute the premium positioning. The discipline of choosing whom to serve and how is called Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning, or STP, and it is the strategic heart of marketing. Segmentation divides the heterogeneous market into groups of similar customers. Targeting picks which segments to serve. Positioning decides what mental space the brand will occupy in the minds of customers in the target segment relative to alternatives. Al Ries and Jack Trout's 1981 book Positioning popularized the central insight: positioning is not about your product, it is about how customers perceive your product compared to alternatives. Volvo positioned around safety, BMW around the ultimate driving experience, Mercedes around luxury heritage, Toyota around reliability. Each owns a position in the buyer's mind that competitors cannot easily attack. This session teaches you to segment markets meaningfully, target segments strategically, and write positioning statements that drive every subsequent marketing decision.
key_concepts: Segmentation bases for consumer markets: geographic (region, climate, urban/rural), demographic (age, gender, income, education, family stage, generation, ethnicity), psychographic (lifestyle, values, personality, VALS framework), behavioral (occasion, benefits sought, user status, usage rate, loyalty, readiness stage, attitude)
B2B segmentation bases: industry vertical, company size, geographic location, technology adoption stage, purchase approach (centralized vs decentralized), urgency, application, customer capabilities

Segment evaluation criteria (Kotler): measurable (size and purchasing power can be quantified), substantial (large and profitable enough to serve), accessible (can be reached through channels), differentiable (responds distinctly to marketing), actionable (firm can design programs to serve)

Targeting strategies: undifferentiated marketing (one offer for whole market, e.g., early Coca-Cola), differentiated marketing (separate offers for multiple segments, e.g., GM with Chevrolet, Buick, Cadillac), concentrated/niche marketing (one segment served deeply, e.g., Ferrari), micromarketing (local or individual customization, e.g., Stitch Fix)

Positioning definition: the act of designing the company's offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the mind of the target market relative to competitors; positioning is about the customer's mind, not the product itself

Points of parity (POP) vs points of difference (POD): POPs are attributes the brand must match to be considered in the category (a credit card must have fraud protection); PODs are attributes that differentiate the brand and give a reason to choose it

Positioning statement template: For [target customer], who [statement of need or opportunity], [brand] is a [product category] that [statement of key benefit]. Unlike [primary competitive alternative], [brand] [statement of primary differentiation]

Perceptual mapping: visualization technique that places brands on 2-3 axes representing key attributes (e.g., price vs quality, traditional vs modern); reveals positioning gaps and competitive crowding

Repositioning: changing the perceived position of a brand; risky and slow because customer perceptions are sticky, but sometimes necessary (Old Spice from 'your grandfather's aftershave' to 'the man your man could smell like' in 2010)

Byron Sharp critique: argues that for FMCG brands, mental and physical availability matter more than differentiation; brands grow by reaching more buyers rather than by serving the same buyers more deeply, and most attempts at distinctive positioning are illusions that customers do not perceive
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a segmentation, targeting, and positioning analysis for the U.S. running shoe market. Segment the market: serious distance runners (training 30+ miles/week, $150+ shoes, brand loyalty to Brooks/Asics/Saucony), recreational fitness runners (5-15 miles/week, $80-130 shoes, brand-curious), casual walkers (1-3 miles/week, $50-100 shoes, comfort-driven), athleisure consumers (do not run, want shoes that look athletic, $80-150, fashion-driven), and price-conscious shoppers (any of the above on a budget). Then position three brands: Brooks targets serious distance runners with the position 'Run Happy - the running specialist that focuses only on running.' Hoka targets recreational and serious runners frustrated with traditional minimalism with the position 'maximalist cushioning that makes long miles feel easier.' On Cloud targets athleisure and recreational runners with the position 'Swiss-engineered Cloud comfort with a distinctive look.' Each brand has chosen a segment, a frame of reference, a point of difference, and a reason to believe. Compare to a brand that tried to be everything: Reebok struggled for years before resetting because it could not articulate who it was for.
setup_requirements: Perceptual map template (Excel or Miro)
Segmentation case study: USAA insurance (military demographic) or Lululemon (yoga psychographic)
Positioning statement template handout
Access to brand websites and ads for the running shoe industry analysis

review.value: Every successful brand has chosen whom to serve and whom to ignore. Trying to be all things to all people produces mediocrity. The hardest part of marketing is saying no to customers who are not your target, but that discipline is what creates strong brands

review.key: Positioning Is About Perception, Not Reality
review.value: Positioning is the place your brand occupies in customers' minds, which is shaped as much by competitor positioning, advertising history, and customer experience as by the actual product. Volvo's safety position is reinforced by every ad and every product launch over decades; competitors cannot simply claim it

Byron Sharp's research shows that for many categories, customers do not actually perceive the distinctive benefits brands claim. Mental and physical availability often matter more than positioning. Position to be relevant, not to be uniquely irreplaceable, especially in mature categories

review.key: Write the Positioning Statement Before the Tactics
review.value: Every advertising campaign, product feature, pricing decision, and channel choice should flow from the positioning statement. If your team cannot write a positioning statement they all agree on in 50 words, your tactical work will be incoherent

class_schedule.topic: Product Strategy and the Product Lifecycle
learning_objectives: Distinguish among the three levels of product (core benefit, actual product, augmented product) and apply them to a real example
Map the product lifecycle stages (introduction, growth, maturity, decline) and identify appropriate marketing strategies for each stage
Apply product mix and product line decisions including line stretching, line filling, and line pruning
Evaluate new product development risks using the new product failure rate data and apply stage-gate processes to reduce risk
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 12 - Setting Product Strategy and Ch. 15 - Introducing New Market Offerings
Geoffrey Moore, Crossing the Chasm (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-3 on the technology adoption lifecycle
Clayton Christensen, The Innovator's Dilemma (1997), Ch. 1-2 on disruptive innovation
Harvard Business Review, 'Choosing the Right Customer' (Simons, 2014) on product-customer fit

lecture_notes.introduction: The product is the foundation of the marketing mix because if the product fails to deliver value, no amount of clever pricing, distribution, or promotion can save it. But 'product' is a deceptively simple word. Theodore Levitt taught that customers do not buy products, they buy solutions to problems, and the implication is that every product exists at multiple levels. The core benefit is the fundamental need being served (a hotel room is not a bed, it is a night of safe rest). The actual product is the tangible offering (room, bed, bathroom, TV). The augmented product is everything else that enhances the experience (24-hour room service, loyalty program, mobile check-in, free Wi-Fi, breakfast). Marriott does not compete with Holiday Inn on the actual product alone; it competes on the augmented product, where loyalty, recognition, and experience differentiate. Then there is the question of how products evolve over time. The product lifecycle (PLC) framework, popularized in the 1960s, describes how sales and profits move through introduction (slow growth, losses), growth (rapid sales increase, first profits), maturity (peak sales, intense competition, margin compression), and decline (falling sales). Each stage requires different marketing strategies. Smartphone makers in the introduction phase needed education and trial; in growth they needed differentiation and channel building; in maturity they need cost control and incremental innovation; eventually they will need exit strategies. This session covers product strategy across both levels and stages, plus the brutal economics of new product development, where 70-80 percent of new consumer products fail within two years.
key_concepts: Three levels of product (Kotler): core benefit (the fundamental need served), actual product (features, design, brand name, packaging, quality), augmented product (delivery, installation, warranty, customer service, after-sale support)
Product mix dimensions: width (how many product lines), length (total items across all lines), depth (variants within each line), consistency (how related the lines are in use, production, channels)

Product line decisions: line stretching (downward to lower-priced segments, upward to premium, two-way), line filling (adding items in existing range), line pruning (removing low-performing items to focus resources)

Product lifecycle stages: introduction (slow sales, high cost per customer, no profit, innovator buyers), growth (rapidly rising sales, profit emerges, early adopters), maturity (peak sales, fierce competition, margin compression, mainstream), decline (falling sales and profits, exit decisions)

Marketing mix strategies by PLC stage: introduction emphasizes awareness and trial with skim or penetration pricing; growth emphasizes brand preference and broad distribution; maturity emphasizes differentiation and defending share; decline emphasizes harvesting profits or exiting

Technology adoption lifecycle (Rogers, popularized by Moore): innovators (2.5 percent), early adopters (13.5 percent), early majority (34 percent), late majority (34 percent), laggards (16 percent); each segment has distinct motivations and requires distinct marketing

The chasm (Geoffrey Moore): the gap between early adopters who tolerate incomplete products and the early majority who require whole-product solutions; many tech products die in the chasm because they cannot make the leap from visionary buyers to pragmatist buyers

New product development process: idea generation, idea screening, concept development and testing, marketing strategy development, business analysis, product development, market testing, commercialization

Stage-gate process: structured new product development with formal go/no-go decisions at each stage, used by Procter & Gamble and 3M; reduces failure rate by killing weak ideas early before significant investment

Disruptive innovation (Christensen): incumbent firms fail not because they are bad at managing but because they correctly serve their best customers with sustaining innovations and ignore low-end disruptors who eventually move upmarket and overtake them; Netflix vs Blockbuster, digital cameras vs Kodak, Tesla vs incumbent automakers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map the iPhone through the product lifecycle and the three product levels. Core benefit: a portable communications and computing device that fits in a pocket. Actual product: hardware (display, processor, camera, battery), software (iOS), packaging, brand. Augmented product: App Store ecosystem with millions of apps, AppleCare, Apple Stores for in-person support, trade-in program, ApplePay, iCloud sync, integration with other Apple devices. Lifecycle journey: introduction in 2007 (first model, 6.1M units, education and trial), growth from 2008-2014 (App Store launch, carrier expansion, rapidly rising sales, profit explosion), maturity from 2015 onward (peak sales of 231M units in 2015, slowing growth, larger screen sizes, services revenue becoming the growth driver as hardware sales plateau), and approaching decline as smartphone penetration saturates and replacement cycles lengthen. Apple's response: services pivot (Apple Music, TV+, Fitness+, iCloud) to monetize the installed base when hardware sales stop growing. Compare to BlackBerry, which had the same opportunity in 2007 but failed to recognize the platform shift and ended up exiting the consumer phone business by 2016.
setup_requirements: Product lifecycle case studies (HBR or Insead)
Stage-gate process template
New product failure rate research (Nielsen Innovation Practice or BCG study)
Sample new product development brief

review.value: Most product categories are commoditized at the actual product level; premium prices and customer loyalty come from the augmented product. Apple's hardware specs are similar to Android competitors, but the ecosystem (App Store, iCloud, service network, Apple Pay) makes switching costly and emotionally hard

The marketing mix that works in introduction (heavy education spend, narrow channels, premium price) is wrong for maturity (cost-focused, broad channels, competitive pricing). Brands that fail to evolve their strategy as the lifecycle progresses get caught defending outdated playbooks

Industry data shows 70-80 percent of new consumer products fail within two years. Stage-gate processes, concept testing, and small-scale market testing reduce failure rates by killing bad ideas early. Skipping these steps to 'move fast' is often the most expensive shortcut a company can take

Incumbents fail when they dismiss low-end entrants as too small to matter. Christensen's research shows that disruptors start in unattractive niches, build capability, and then move upmarket to overtake incumbents. Watch every low-end entrant in your market; they may be the next existential threat

learning_objectives: Define brand equity and explain how it creates financial value beyond the underlying product
Apply Aaker's brand equity model (awareness, perceived quality, associations, loyalty) to evaluate a real brand
Apply Keller's Brand Resonance Model (CBBE pyramid: salience, performance, imagery, judgments, feelings, resonance)
Identify the marketing decisions that build, leverage, and protect brand equity over time
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 10 - Crafting the Brand Positioning and Ch. 11 - Creating Brand Equity
David Aaker, Managing Brand Equity (1991), Ch. 1-3 - foundational text on brand equity
Kevin Lane Keller, Strategic Brand Management (5th ed.), Ch. 2 on the CBBE model
Harvard Business Review, 'The Brand Report Card' (Keller, 2000) for brand audit framework

lecture_notes.introduction: Why does a Coca-Cola sell for 50 percent more than a private-label cola that tastes essentially the same? Why do customers pay $1,200 for an iPhone when comparable Android phones cost $400? Why does the LVMH conglomerate have a market capitalization larger than the GDP of many countries? The answer is brand equity, the additional value created by the brand name beyond the value of the underlying product or service. Interbrand's annual rankings put Apple's brand value at over $500 billion in 2024, larger than the entire economies of 150+ countries. Brand equity is not magic; it is a measurable asset built over years through consistent product experience, emotional resonance, and strategic communication. David Aaker's foundational work in the early 1990s defined brand equity as having four dimensions: awareness (do people know the brand exists?), perceived quality (do they believe it is good?), brand associations (what attributes and feelings come to mind?), and loyalty (will they buy it again and recommend it?). Kevin Lane Keller built on this with the Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) pyramid, which traces a brand's growth from simple awareness through performance and imagery to the deepest possible relationship of resonance, where the brand becomes part of the customer's identity. Apple, Harley-Davidson, Patagonia, and Nike have achieved this top of the pyramid for many of their customers. This session teaches you to evaluate brand equity, identify the marketing decisions that build it, and avoid the actions that destroy it.
key_concepts: Brand equity definition: the differential effect that brand knowledge has on consumer response to the marketing of the brand; in financial terms, the premium price and loyalty a branded product earns over an unbranded equivalent

Aaker's brand equity dimensions (1991): brand awareness (recognition, recall), perceived quality (functional and emotional), brand associations (attributes, benefits, attitudes, organization, country, celebrity), brand loyalty (behavioral and attitudinal)
Keller's Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) pyramid: salience (who are you?), performance and imagery (what are you?), judgments and feelings (what about you?), resonance (what about you and me?)

Brand identity vs brand image: identity is what the brand wants to be (controlled by the firm), image is what customers actually perceive (in the customer's head); the gap between identity and image is the brand challenge
Brand elements: name, logo, symbol, character, slogan, jingle, packaging, URL; each can be evaluated on memorability, meaningfulness, likability, transferability, adaptability, protectability

Brand architecture decisions: branded house (Apple uses the Apple brand on all products), house of brands (P&G uses Tide, Crest, Pampers as separate brands), endorsed brands (Marriott Courtyard, Marriott Residence Inn), sub-brands (Toyota Camry, Toyota Corolla)

Brand extensions: leveraging an established brand into new categories (Apple from computers to phones to watches to services); successful extensions reinforce the parent brand, failed extensions dilute it (Harley-Davidson cologne, Bic underwear)
Co-branding and ingredient branding: Intel Inside, Nike + Apple, GoPro on red Bull videos; works when both brands gain associations and target overlapping audiences

Brand valuation methods: cost-based (what it would cost to recreate the brand), market-based (what comparable brands sell for in M&A transactions), income-based (NPV of brand-attributable cash flows); Interbrand and Brand Finance publish annual rankings using income-based methods

Brand crises and recovery: Tylenol cyanide tampering 1982 (Johnson & Johnson recall response became the gold standard), United Breaks Guitars 2009 (slow response damaged the brand), Volkswagen diesel emissions 2015 (years of damage), Pepsi Kendall Jenner ad 2017 (pulled within 24 hours)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the CBBE pyramid to Apple as a brand audit. Salience: Apple has near-100 percent brand awareness in developed markets and scores high on category recall (when asked about smartphones, computers, or earbuds, Apple comes to mind). Performance: customers consistently rate Apple high on product quality, design, ease of use, and reliability (J.D. Power and Consumer Reports both rate iPhone customer satisfaction at the top of the smartphone category). Imagery: Apple is associated with creativity, sophistication, premium quality, status, simplicity, and innovation (the 'Think Different' campaign and Steve Jobs's keynotes built these associations over decades). Judgments: customers rate Apple highly on quality, credibility, consideration, and superiority. Feelings: warmth (the personal stories in Apple ads), fun, excitement, security (encryption commitments), self-respect (using Apple is a statement of identity), social approval. Resonance: Apple users exhibit behavioral loyalty (90+ percent retention rate), attitudinal attachment (the brand is part of identity for many), sense of community (Apple Stores as gathering places, online forums), active engagement (fans line up for product launches and produce content about the brand). Apple sits at the top of the pyramid for millions of customers, which translates into pricing power, premium margins, and predictable repeat purchases.
setup_requirements: Aaker brand equity book or summary handout
Keller CBBE pyramid template
Interbrand Best Global Brands 2024 ranking
Brand audit case study (HBR Volkswagen Diesel Crisis or J&J Tylenol)

review.key: Brand Equity Is a Real Asset
review.value: Brand equity is not soft or unmeasurable; it shows up in pricing power, customer retention, channel access, and acquisition multiples in M&A. Apple's brand alone is valued at over $500 billion. Treating brand investment as discretionary expense rather than capital investment is a strategic mistake

Brand equity is built through years of consistent product experience and consistent communication. Brands that change direction every few years (rebranding, repositioning, new agencies) confuse customers and erode equity. The strongest brands hold core elements stable for decades while evolving execution

review.key: Crises Are Survivable If Handled Well
review.value: Even severe brand crises (Tylenol cyanide deaths, Toyota unintended acceleration) can be survived if the company responds quickly, transparently, and prioritizes customer safety over short-term financials. Crises that are mishandled (United, Wells Fargo, Boeing 737 MAX) inflict damage that takes years to repair

Each brand extension carries the risk of diluting the parent brand if the new product fails or contradicts existing associations. Extensions should reinforce, not contradict, what the brand already stands for. Harley-Davidson cologne and Cosmopolitan yogurt are textbook examples of damaging extensions

learning_objectives: Compare cost-based, competition-based, and value-based pricing approaches and identify when each applies
Apply price elasticity of demand to predict revenue impact of price changes and identify low-elasticity opportunities
Distinguish among new product pricing strategies (skim vs penetration) and product mix pricing strategies (line, optional, captive, by-product, bundle)
Recognize and apply psychological pricing tactics including reference pricing, charm pricing, prestige pricing, and price framing
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 16 - Developing Pricing Strategies and Programs
Thomas Nagle and Georg Muller, The Strategy and Tactics of Pricing (6th ed.), Ch. 1-4
Marn, Roegner & Zawada, The Price Advantage (2nd ed.) - McKinsey-developed pricing reference
Harvard Business Review, 'Pricing to Create Shared Value' (Bertini & Koenigsberg, 2014)

lecture_notes.introduction: Of the four Ps, price is the only one that produces revenue; the others are costs. A 1 percent improvement in price typically improves operating profit by 8-11 percent for typical companies, far more than equivalent improvements in volume, variable cost, or fixed cost (McKinsey research). Yet pricing is often the least disciplined element of the marketing mix. Many companies set prices using cost-plus formulas (calculate cost, add desired margin, set price), which guarantees that you capture none of the value above cost. Or they price by competitor parity (look at competitors and match), which surrenders pricing power to the rival's decisions. Value-based pricing, the discipline of setting price based on what the offering is worth to the customer, is harder but produces dramatically better results. Apple's iPhone gross margins of 38-42 percent are not because the phone is cheaper to make than competitors, they are because Apple captures more of the value it creates through positioning, ecosystem lock-in, and willingness to charge premium prices to customers who value the brand. Pricing also has a behavioral dimension that pure economics misses. The same product is perceived differently at $9.99 versus $10.00, at a list price with a discount versus the same final price without a discount, when bundled with another item versus sold separately. This session covers strategic pricing approaches, pricing tactics, and the behavioral psychology that explains why the same number can produce very different sales results.
key_concepts: Three pricing approaches: cost-based (cost plus markup), competition-based (set by reference to competitor prices), value-based (set by reference to customer's willingness to pay based on value delivered); value-based produces highest margins but requires deep customer understanding

Price elasticity of demand: percent change in quantity demanded divided by percent change in price; elastic (>1) means demand falls more than price rises (commodities), inelastic (<1) means demand falls less (insulin, gasoline short-run, premium brands)
Six steps in pricing (Kotler): select pricing objective, determine demand, estimate costs, analyze competitors' costs/prices/offers, select pricing method, select final price

Pricing objectives: survival (covering variable costs), maximum current profit, maximum market share (penetration pricing), maximum market skimming (premium price for early adopters), product-quality leadership (premium positioning)

New product pricing strategies: market-skimming pricing (high price to maximize per-unit profit from price-insensitive innovators, then drop over time, e.g., new iPhone models), market-penetration pricing (low price to gain share quickly, e.g., Amazon Kindle, Costco)

Product mix pricing: product line pricing (price steps within a category, e.g., good-better-best), optional product pricing (base price plus paid options, e.g., car options), captive product pricing (cheap base with expensive consumables, e.g., razors and blades, printers and ink), two-part pricing (membership plus usage, e.g., Costco), by-product pricing (revenue from by-products allows lower price on main product), product bundle pricing (lower combined price than separate components)

Price discrimination: charging different prices for the same product to different segments based on willingness to pay; legal forms include customer segment (student discounts), product form (basic vs premium), location (movie theater seats), time (matinee vs evening, peak vs off-peak), and quantity (volume discounts)

Psychological pricing tactics: reference prices (the comparison anchor), charm pricing ($9.99 vs $10.00), prestige pricing (high price as quality signal), price framing (per day vs per year), decoy pricing (the third option that makes the second look reasonable), price anchoring (showing original price next to sale)

Price wars: competitive price reductions that spiral until margins collapse; usually destructive to all participants and rarely yield lasting share shifts; airline industry, US automakers, and consumer electronics have lost billions to price wars

EDLP vs Hi-Lo: everyday low pricing (Walmart, ALDI) maintains consistent low prices and avoids promotion cycles; high-low pricing (department stores, supermarkets) uses regular prices with frequent promotions to drive traffic and create urgency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through an Apple iPhone pricing strategy decomposition. The iPhone 15 Pro launches at $999 and the iPhone 15 Pro Max at $1,199. Why this pricing? Cost analysis: bill of materials is approximately $400-500, so a cost-plus 30 percent markup would price the phone at $520-650. Apple charges $999, capturing roughly $500 in incremental margin per unit beyond what cost-plus would yield. This is value-based pricing: Apple has identified that loyal customers will pay this premium for the brand, ecosystem, and design. Then notice the product line pricing: iPhone 15 at $799, 15 Plus at $899, 15 Pro at $999, 15 Pro Max at $1,199. The $200 step from Plus to Pro and $200 from Pro to Pro Max are deliberate; the difference in cost to Apple is much less than $200, so the premium tiers are highly profitable. The Pro Max at $1,199 also serves as an anchor that makes the $999 Pro feel reasonable. Captive pricing: AppleCare, iCloud storage, App Store revenue share, and Apple Music subscriptions all generate ongoing revenue from each iPhone customer (services revenue is now Apple's largest growth driver). Skimming over time: this year's $999 iPhone becomes next year's $799 model and the year after's $599 entry-level option, capturing willingness to pay across the whole curve. Compare to Samsung, which uses a similar tier strategy but with less brand premium and lower margins because the value-perception gap is smaller.
setup_requirements: Price elasticity calculator template
Apple iPhone pricing history chart (2007-present)
Sample pricing case studies: Netflix tiers, Spotify tiers, AWS services pricing
Behavioral pricing reference (Dan Ariely Predictably Irrational chapters on pricing)

review.value: Cost-plus pricing guarantees you capture only your desired margin above cost, leaving all extra value on the table. Value-based pricing requires understanding customer willingness to pay but produces dramatically higher margins. Apple, Tesla, and Patagonia are masters of value-based pricing; commodity producers are stuck with cost-plus

review.key: Small Price Changes Have Large Profit Impact
review.value: McKinsey research shows that a 1 percent price improvement typically yields 8-11 percent operating profit improvement, far more than equivalent volume or cost changes. Pricing should be a board-level strategic discussion, not an afterthought left to junior product managers

Reference prices, anchoring, charm pricing, and bundling all work even though they have no rational basis in classical economics. Customers do not have stable internal valuations; they construct preferences in the moment based on how options are presented. Use this to your advantage in price design

Price wars are almost always destructive to all participants and rarely produce lasting share shifts. Find dimensions of competition (product features, brand, service) where you can win without dragging the entire industry's profitability down. If a competitor cuts price, do not automatically match; respond with non-price moves first

learning_objectives: Identify the functions of marketing channels (information, promotion, contact, matching, negotiation, physical distribution, financing, risk-taking)
Compare channel structures: direct, single-tier, multi-tier; and channel intensity options: intensive, selective, exclusive distribution
Recognize sources of channel conflict (vertical, horizontal, multichannel) and apply techniques to manage them
Evaluate the strategic role of retailers, wholesalers, and direct-to-consumer channels in modern marketing
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 17 - Designing and Managing Integrated Marketing Channels
Anne Coughlan et al., Marketing Channels (8th ed.), Ch. 1-3
Harvard Business Review, 'Channel Stuffing and the Margin of Safety' (Healy, 2003)
Harvard Business Review, 'How to Win in Channels' (Friedman, 2017)

lecture_notes.introduction: A great product priced correctly will still fail if customers cannot buy it where, when, and how they want. Distribution is the unglamorous but decisive element of the marketing mix; it determines whether your brand is on the shelf when the customer walks in, in the search results when the customer searches, in the recommended algorithm when the customer browses. Coca-Cola's stated goal for decades has been that the brand should be 'within an arm's reach of desire,' which is a distribution strategy: be everywhere a thirsty person might be, in vending machines, restaurants, gas stations, grocery stores, stadiums, hotels, and offices. That distribution intensity is a competitive moat that no startup can match easily. Conversely, when Nike decided in 2017 to dramatically reduce its wholesale distribution (cutting 50 percent of retail partners by 2022 and going direct-to-consumer through Nike.com, the Nike app, and Nike-owned stores), it was making a strategic bet that its brand was strong enough to survive the lost shelf space and that direct customer relationships would be more valuable than wholesale revenue. The bet went poorly: by 2024, Nike had to reverse course, restoring wholesale partnerships after losing share to upstart brands like Hoka and On Running that had taken the freed shelf space. This session covers how channels work, what they do for the brand, why conflict is inevitable when multiple channels exist, and how the rise of e-commerce and D2C has restructured the channel landscape across nearly every category.
key_concepts: Channel functions (Kotler): information gathering, promotion, contact (finding and communicating with prospective buyers), matching (customizing the offer to buyer needs), negotiation, physical distribution, financing, risk-taking; the question is not whether these functions get performed but who in the channel performs them

Channel levels: zero-level (direct from producer to consumer, e.g., D2C), one-level (one intermediary, e.g., manufacturer to retailer to consumer), two-level (manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to consumer), three-level (adds jobbers between wholesaler and retailer)

Channel intensity decisions: intensive distribution (maximize coverage, e.g., Coca-Cola, Frito-Lay), selective distribution (limited number of retailers in each market, e.g., Apple in approved Apple Authorized Resellers), exclusive distribution (one retailer per territory, e.g., luxury goods, car dealerships)

Push vs pull strategy: push uses sales force and trade promotion to push the product through channels to consumers; pull uses advertising and consumer promotion to create demand that pulls the product through channels (consumers ask retailers, retailers ask wholesalers, wholesalers ask manufacturers)

Channel conflict types: vertical conflict (between levels in the same channel, e.g., manufacturer vs retailer over pricing), horizontal conflict (between members at the same level, e.g., two retailers in the same area), multichannel conflict (conflict between different channels, e.g., manufacturer's website vs physical retail partners)

Vertical Marketing Systems (VMS): corporate VMS (single ownership of multiple channel levels, e.g., Apple owning Apple Stores), administered VMS (one channel member with size advantage coordinates the others, e.g., Walmart imposing standards on suppliers), contractual VMS (franchise systems like McDonald's, retailer cooperatives like Ace Hardware)

Retailer types: department stores (Macy's), specialty stores (Lululemon), supermarkets (Kroger), convenience stores (7-Eleven), discount stores (Target), warehouse clubs (Costco), category killers (Best Buy in earlier era), off-price retailers (TJ Maxx), online-only retailers (Amazon), direct-to-consumer brand stores (Warby Parker)

Wholesaler functions: bulk-breaking, assortment, market coverage, financing, risk-bearing, market intelligence; wholesalers add value when their efficiency in performing these functions exceeds what manufacturers and retailers could do themselves

E-commerce disruption: Amazon's rise has restructured retail by enabling long-tail product availability, comparison shopping, and same-day delivery; legacy retailers (Sears, RadioShack, Circuit City, Toys R Us, Bed Bath & Beyond) that failed to adapt have collapsed

D2C economics: direct channels capture full margin and enable customer data ownership, but require building marketing capability, fulfillment infrastructure, and customer service; the dollar shave club model worked because the underlying product economics could absorb high CAC, while many D2C brands have failed when paid acquisition costs rose and unit economics broke
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare three channel strategies in the eyewear industry. Traditional Luxottica model: vertically integrated giant manufactures and owns Ray-Ban, Oakley, Persol, plus the LensCrafters and Sunglass Hut retail chains, plus licenses for Chanel, Prada, Burberry frames; a one-stop monopoly on premium eyewear with 80+ percent market share in some segments. Warby Parker D2C model: founded in 2010 with the explicit thesis that eyewear was overpriced because of Luxottica's vertical control; Warby Parker designed their own frames, sourced manufacturing directly, sold online through Warby Parker.com with a home try-on program, and gradually opened owned retail stores; bypassed the entire wholesale/retail layer and offered $95 prescription glasses versus the $300+ industry average. Zenni Optical pure-online model: even cheaper at $20-50 per pair, no physical stores, leverages low-cost manufacturing in China, accepts longer delivery times. Each model trades off something: Luxottica gets premium brand control and store presence at high cost; Warby Parker gets brand ownership and customer data with moderate cost; Zenni gets rock-bottom prices but minimal brand. The lesson: channel design is a strategic choice that determines what kind of business you can be.
setup_requirements: Channel mapping template
Case studies: Nike DTC strategy 2017-2024, Warby Parker founding story, Apple Store retail strategy
Wholesale vs DTC unit economics calculator
Retail segmentation framework reference

review.value: Brands with broad, deep, well-managed distribution have advantages that startups cannot easily match. Coca-Cola's distribution to millions of points of sale globally is a barrier to entry that even very well-funded competitors cannot replicate quickly. Distribution moats compound over decades

Any time a manufacturer sells through multiple channels (wholesale plus DTC, physical plus online, multiple retailers), conflict arises because the channels compete for the same customers. The goal is not to eliminate conflict but to manage it through pricing rules, exclusive products, and clear policies

Going direct sounds attractive because it captures full margin and customer data, but it shifts the cost of marketing, fulfillment, and customer service from the retailer to the manufacturer. Many D2C startups have failed because they underestimated these costs and overestimated their ability to acquire customers profitably at scale

The right channel depends on how customers want to buy. High-consideration purchases (cars, houses, business software) often need consultative selling channels. Low-consideration replenishment purchases (paper towels, toothpaste) need ubiquitous availability. Understand the buying process before designing the channel

class_schedule.topic: Integrated Marketing Communications: Advertising, PR, and Promotions
learning_objectives: Identify the elements of the marketing communications mix and select appropriate tools for different objectives
Apply the AIDA hierarchy of effects model and the more recent ELM (Elaboration Likelihood Model) to plan persuasive communication
Design a media plan with consideration of reach, frequency, GRPs, and CPM as planning metrics
Distinguish among advertising, public relations, sales promotion, direct marketing, personal selling, and events as IMC tools and identify when each is most effective
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 19 - Designing and Managing Integrated Marketing Communications and Ch. 20 - Mass Communications
Don Schultz, Stanley Tannenbaum, Robert Lauterborn, Integrated Marketing Communications (1993) - founding text on IMC
Les Binet and Peter Field, The Long and the Short of It (IPA, 2013) - on the 60/40 brand-to-activation budget split
David Ogilvy, Ogilvy on Advertising (1983) - classic creative principles

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing communications is how the brand talks to customers, and every brand says something whether or not it intends to.
