Silence is also a message; an outdated website, an ignored social channel, and absence from review sites all say things about the brand. The discipline of Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) is the recognition that customers do not separate ads from PR from social posts from product packaging from store experiences; they form one impression of the brand from all these touchpoints combined. If the messages contradict each other, the brand becomes incoherent. Don Schultz coined the phrase IMC in the early 1990s to push back against the silos of advertising, PR, sales promotion, and direct marketing, which were typically managed by separate teams or agencies with no coordination. Today, the proliferation of digital channels has multiplied the silos, and the integration challenge is harder than ever. A brand might have one team buying TV, another buying paid social, another writing email campaigns, another running influencer programs, another handling PR, another producing content, and another managing customer support. If they are not coordinated around a single positioning and consistent voice, customers see contradictions. This session covers the IMC mix, the persuasive psychology behind effective communication, and the planning frameworks (AIDA, ELM, Binet and Field) that help marketers spend their budgets where they create the most value.
key_concepts: IMC mix: advertising (paid mass communication), sales promotion (short-term incentives like coupons, contests, samples), public relations (earned media and corporate communications), direct marketing (one-to-one through mail, email, mobile, telemarketing), personal selling (face-to-face by sales force), events and experiences (sponsorships, festivals, trade shows), interactive marketing (websites, apps, social), word-of-mouth marketing
AIDA hierarchy of effects: Attention (catch the eye), Interest (engage the mind), Desire (build emotional connection), Action (prompt purchase); a 1898 framework that still guides creative briefs

Elaboration Likelihood Model (Petty and Cacioppo): two routes to persuasion: central route (high involvement, customer processes arguments deeply, requires strong rational arguments) vs peripheral route (low involvement, customer relies on cues like attractiveness, celebrity endorsement, source credibility); match the route to the audience and product

Reach vs frequency tradeoff: reach is the percent of target audience exposed at least once in a period; frequency is the average number of exposures; for any fixed budget, you can have high reach with low frequency or vice versa; effective frequency theory suggests 3+ exposures are needed to register
GRPs (Gross Rating Points) = reach x frequency; the standard metric for TV and radio media planning; CPM (cost per thousand impressions) is the standard metric for comparing media efficiency

Creative principles (David Ogilvy): the headline does most of the work, sell the product not the cleverness, tell the truth but make it interesting, big ideas trump small ideas, brand personality must be consistent over decades

Public relations functions: press relations, product publicity, corporate communications, lobbying, counseling, crisis management; PR earns coverage rather than buying it, which makes it more credible but less controllable

Sales promotion types: consumer promotions (samples, coupons, premiums, contests, loyalty programs), trade promotions (allowances, contests, push money), business promotions (trade shows, conventions, contests for sales force); promotions move sales forward but rarely build long-term equity
Native advertising and content marketing: paid placements designed to look like editorial content; effective when transparent, deceptive when not; the FTC requires clear disclosure

Binet and Field 60/40 framework: research across 1000+ campaigns showed that brands grow most when they invest 60 percent in long-term brand-building communications (emotional, broad reach) and 40 percent in short-term sales activation (rational, targeted, conversion-focused); most brands invert this ratio and over-invest in activation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Decompose the Nike Colin Kaepernick 'Dream Crazy' campaign from 2018. Strategy: Nike chose a polarizing cultural figure to reinforce its 'Just Do It' brand position around standing for what you believe even when it costs you something. IMC integration: TV spot featuring Kaepernick's voiceover ran during NFL games (massive reach to target audience), simultaneously released on YouTube where it accumulated 80M+ views, billboards in major cities, social media amplification, in-store displays. PR: Nike anticipated the controversy and prepared for it; news coverage of the campaign reached more people than the actual ads, generating an estimated $163 million in earned media in the first 48 hours. Sales activation: Nike.com checkout, Nike app, and SNKRS app had Kaepernick-related products ready to ship. Outcome: stock dropped 3 percent in the first 24 hours as some customers boycotted, but online sales surged 31 percent in the following week, and sales overall increased 10 percent in the quarter. Brand metrics: Nike's brand equity scores rose among the target Gen Z and Millennial segment even as they fell among older conservative consumers, exactly as the strategy intended. The campaign worked because all elements (advertising, PR, in-store, digital, retail) were coordinated around a single message and a clear understanding of the target audience. Compare to Pepsi's failed Kendall Jenner ad in 2017 which lacked this integration and was pulled within 24 hours.
setup_requirements: Sample media plan template with reach/frequency/GRP/CPM calculations
Case studies: Nike Dream Crazy campaign, Apple Think Different, Always Like a Girl
Binet and Field IPA report on long and short of marketing effectiveness
Sample creative brief template

review.value: Customers experience all your communications as one impression of the brand. If your TV ads say one thing, your packaging says another, and your social media says a third, you confuse customers. IMC is not optional; it is the minimum standard for not wasting your communications budget

Binet and Field's research is clear: brands that invest only in short-term activation (paid search, retargeting, promotions) eventually run out of demand to capture because they are not creating new demand. The 60/40 brand-to-activation split is the proven path to sustained growth

review.key: Match the Route to the Audience
review.value: High-involvement decisions (B2B software, cars, mortgages) require central-route persuasion with strong rational arguments and proof points. Low-involvement decisions (snacks, beverages, consumer staples) work through peripheral cues like attractiveness, repetition, and emotional resonance. Do not confuse the two

When a campaign generates news coverage, social shares, and word of mouth, the effective reach is multiplied beyond what the paid budget alone could achieve. Plan for earned media from the start by building campaigns around ideas that are inherently shareable, talkable, or controversial

class_schedule.topic: Digital Marketing Introduction: Bridge to Modern Channels
learning_objectives: Map the major digital marketing channels (search, social, display, email, content, mobile, video) and their distinct strategic roles
Distinguish owned, earned, and paid media and explain why most modern brands need all three
Apply digital metrics (CTR, CPC, CPM, conversion rate, ROAS) to evaluate channel performance
Understand the privacy and platform shifts (iOS 14.5 ATT, third-party cookie deprecation, GDPR/CCPA) that have reshaped digital marketing in the 2020s
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 21 - Personal Communications
Chaffey & Ellis-Chadwick, Digital Marketing: Strategy, Implementation and Practice (8th ed.), Ch. 1-2
Kotler, Kartajaya & Setiawan, Marketing 5.0: Technology for Humanity, Ch. 1-3
Smart Insights, 'Digital Marketing Strategy and Planning Toolkit'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital marketing is not a separate discipline; it is the dominant set of channels through which modern marketing happens. Global digital ad spending exceeded $740 billion in 2024, surpassing all traditional media combined. A marketer who cannot operate in digital channels is as obsolete as one who could not write a press release in 1980. This session is the bridge between the foundational marketing concepts you have learned in BA201 (segmentation, positioning, brand, IMC) and the digital execution that is the focus of BA304 Digital Marketing. The goal here is not to make you an expert in any single digital channel but to give you the strategic literacy to understand how the channels fit together, what each does well, and how to evaluate their performance. The strategic frameworks you have learned still apply: STP determines who you are targeting, brand positioning shapes the messages, and the marketing concept demands that you create more value than competitors. Digital channels are tactical execution layers, not replacements for strategy. The brands that win digital are the ones that bring strong strategic foundations to the new channels. The brands that fail digital usually fail because they treat it as a tactical add-on managed by junior staff, or because they assume the algorithms will compensate for weak strategy. Neither works.
key_concepts: Major digital channel categories: search (Google, Bing search ads and SEO), paid social (Meta, TikTok, LinkedIn, X, Snap, Pinterest), display (programmatic banner ads, Google Display Network), video (YouTube, connected TV), email and SMS, content marketing (blog, video, podcast), affiliate marketing, influencer marketing, mobile (in-app ads, push notifications, mobile web)

Owned, earned, paid media framework: owned is what the brand controls (website, app, email list, physical retail), earned is third-party endorsement (PR coverage, organic social, customer reviews, word of mouth), paid is media bought directly (search ads, social ads, display); most brands need all three working together

The funnel in digital terms: awareness (paid social, display, video, influencer), consideration (search, content, retargeting), conversion (paid search on transactional terms, retargeting, email), retention (email, app push, loyalty), advocacy (organic social, reviews, referrals)

Search marketing: Google captures over 90 percent of search market share globally (excluding China); SEO is organic search visibility, SEM/PPC is paid search; both target users with active intent, the highest-value audience in digital

Paid social mechanics: auction-based bidding for impressions, targeting by demographics, interests, behaviors, and lookalikes of existing customers; algorithm optimization toward conversion events selected by the marketer

Email marketing: highest-ROI digital channel by Direct Marketing Association estimates ($36-42 return per $1 spent), based on owned audience, requires opt-in (CAN-SPAM, GDPR), automation (welcome series, abandoned cart, post-purchase), and segmentation

Mobile marketing: mobile web vs in-app, push notifications, in-app advertising, SMS, location-based marketing; mobile is over 60 percent of digital media time and the primary device for younger demographics

Key digital metrics: impressions, clicks, CTR (clicks/impressions), CPC (cost per click), CPM (cost per thousand impressions), conversion rate (conversions/clicks), CPA (cost per acquisition), ROAS (return on ad spend = revenue/spend), LTV:CAC ratio

Privacy and platform shifts: iOS 14.5 App Tracking Transparency (2021) limited cross-app tracking and devastated Meta's targeting precision; Chrome's third-party cookie deprecation (delayed but coming); GDPR (EU 2018), CCPA (California 2020), and similar laws worldwide require consent for data collection

First-party data strategy: as third-party tracking becomes harder, brands that own first-party data through email lists, account creation, and direct customer relationships have a structural advantage over those dependent on third-party platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map a complete digital strategy for a hypothetical D2C skincare brand at $5M annual revenue. Owned: Shopify website with strong SEO foundation, Klaviyo email list of 50K subscribers segmented by purchase history and engagement, Instagram with 80K followers, TikTok with 30K, branded blog with 50 educational articles, mobile app for repeat customers. Earned: PR coverage in beauty publications, customer reviews on Yotpo, organic Instagram and TikTok content, influencer mentions, affiliate program. Paid: Meta and TikTok ads targeting lookalikes of best customers and broad interest audiences ($60K/month), Google Ads on branded and category terms ($15K/month), retargeting through Meta and Google ($10K/month), Pinterest for top-of-funnel ($5K/month). Total paid budget: $90K/month or $1.08M annually (about 22 percent of revenue, on the high end but typical for growth-stage D2C). KPIs to track: monthly new customer acquisition, blended CAC, paid ROAS by channel, email revenue, retention rate at 90 and 180 days, repeat purchase rate, average order value, gross margin percentage. Then identify the strategic risks: over-dependence on Meta (a single platform change could crater the model), weak email program (only 25 percent of subscribers active in last 90 days), no scaling content production. Recommend specific shifts to balance the channel mix and reduce platform risk.
setup_requirements: Google Analytics Demo account access
Meta Ads Library for analyzing real ads
Klaviyo or Mailchimp free tier account
Spreadsheet for in-class digital marketing math exercises

review.value: Treating digital as a separate discipline managed by a separate team with separate strategy guarantees inconsistency and underperformance. Modern marketing leaders integrate digital into all marketing planning from the start rather than tacking it on at the end

The brands winning digital are the ones that bring strong segmentation, positioning, and brand foundations to the new channels. Without strategy, digital becomes a series of disconnected tactical experiments that consume budget without producing durable results

Platforms can change algorithms, raise prices, restrict targeting, or disappear entirely. Email lists, app users, and direct customer relationships are owned assets that survive platform changes. Build owned audience aggressively as protection against platform risk

iOS 14.5 was not a temporary disruption; it was the start of a long-term shift toward consumer privacy and platform restrictions on tracking. The next decade of digital marketing will reward brands that built first-party data strategies and punish those that depended on third-party tracking

class_schedule.topic: Services Marketing and the SERVQUAL Framework
learning_objectives: Identify the four characteristics that distinguish services from goods (intangibility, inseparability, variability, perishability) and the marketing implications of each
Apply the services marketing triangle (company, employee, customer) to align internal, external, and interactive marketing
Apply the SERVQUAL model and the five service-quality dimensions (reliability, responsiveness, assurance, empathy, tangibles)
Diagnose service failures using the GAP model and design service recovery procedures
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 13 - Designing and Managing Services
Valarie Zeithaml, Mary Jo Bitner, Dwayne Gremler, Services Marketing (7th ed.), Ch. 1-3, 4, 13
Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 'A Conceptual Model of Service Quality and Its Implications for Future Research,' Journal of Marketing (1985)
Heskett, Sasser & Schlesinger, The Service Profit Chain (1997)

lecture_notes.introduction: Services account for over 80 percent of GDP in developed economies and a similar share of employment, yet most marketing textbooks were written about manufactured goods and services were treated as an afterthought. Services are fundamentally different from goods in ways that change every element of the marketing mix. You cannot stockpile a haircut or a hotel night; you cannot guarantee that two visits to the same restaurant will deliver identical experiences; you cannot inspect a consulting engagement before purchase the way you can inspect a car. Valarie Zeithaml and Mary Jo Bitner's foundational work in the 1980s and 1990s built the field of services marketing by identifying the four distinguishing characteristics (intangibility, inseparability of production and consumption, variability, perishability) and developing frameworks like the services marketing triangle, the SERVQUAL scale, and the GAP model that diagnose where service quality breaks down. The Service Profit Chain research at Harvard Business School showed that service businesses produce profit through a chain that starts with employee satisfaction and capability, leads to service quality, drives customer satisfaction and loyalty, and ends in revenue and profit growth. Brands that understand this chain (Southwest Airlines, Marriott, Disney, Trader Joe's) invest in employees as the source of brand experience; brands that ignore it (every airline that has cut training and staffing to reduce costs) suffer customer revolts and service collapses. This session covers the unique challenges of marketing services and the frameworks that help diagnose and improve them.
key_concepts: Four service characteristics: intangibility (cannot be seen, touched, smelled, or tested before purchase), inseparability (produced and consumed simultaneously, often with the customer present), variability (quality depends on who provides it, when, where, and to whom), perishability (cannot be stored for later sale or use)

Marketing implications of intangibility: customers cannot evaluate before purchase, so they look for tangible cues (facility, staff, equipment, branding); services marketers must 'tangibilize the intangible' through visible signals of quality

Marketing implications of inseparability: the service provider is part of the product, the customer is part of the production process, and other customers can affect the experience (a noisy patron at a quiet restaurant); employee training and customer education both matter
Marketing implications of variability: standardization is difficult; quality control happens in real time during the service encounter; complaint handling is critical because problems are inevitable

Marketing implications of perishability: capacity must be matched to demand because unused capacity is lost forever; airlines, hotels, restaurants use yield management to fill capacity; service providers use part-time staff and shift scheduling to flex capacity

Services marketing triangle: company, employees, customers; three legs are external marketing (company to customer, the promises), internal marketing (company to employees, training and motivation to deliver promises), and interactive marketing (employees to customers, the actual delivery of promises)

SERVQUAL five dimensions of service quality (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry): reliability (delivering as promised), responsiveness (willingness to help promptly), assurance (knowledge and courtesy that inspire trust), empathy (caring individualized attention), tangibles (physical facilities, equipment, appearance of staff)

GAP model of service quality: Gap 1 (knowledge gap, between customer expectations and management perception), Gap 2 (standards gap, between management perception and service quality specifications), Gap 3 (delivery gap, between specifications and actual delivery), Gap 4 (communications gap, between delivery and what was promised externally), Gap 5 (the customer gap, between expectations and perceptions)

Service recovery paradox: customers who experience a problem that is recovered well can end up more loyal than customers who never had a problem; this is why excellent recovery procedures are an investment, not a cost

Service Profit Chain (Heskett, Sasser, Schlesinger): internal service quality leads to employee satisfaction, which leads to employee retention and productivity, which leads to external service quality, which leads to customer satisfaction and loyalty, which leads to revenue growth and profitability; the chain is empirically validated across many service industries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the SERVQUAL and GAP frameworks to two contrasting hotel experiences: Ritz-Carlton vs a budget chain. Ritz-Carlton's commitment is captured in its credo: 'We are Ladies and Gentlemen serving Ladies and Gentlemen.' Reliability: rooms are always ready as promised, requests are tracked and remembered (the employee preference system stores guest preferences across global properties so a guest who requested a foam pillow in Tokyo gets one in Paris). Responsiveness: every employee is empowered to spend up to $2,000 per guest to resolve a problem without manager approval. Assurance: extensive employee training (over 250 hours per year for service staff) creates confident, knowledgeable interactions. Empathy: staff are trained to anticipate needs (offering an umbrella before being asked when it starts raining). Tangibles: marble lobbies, fresh flowers, branded amenities. Now contrast with a budget chain: rooms sometimes not ready at check-in time (reliability gap), front desk staff under-trained (assurance gap), no empowerment to resolve issues (responsiveness gap), generic transactional interactions (empathy gap), worn furnishings (tangibles gap). Both target different segments and price accordingly, but customers at Ritz-Carlton pay 5-10x the price for a service experience that is fundamentally different on every SERVQUAL dimension. Then walk through a specific service recovery: a guest at the Ritz-Carlton finds a stain on the carpet at check-in. Recovery script: immediate sincere apology, room change, a written note from the GM, an upgraded room or suite for the inconvenience, and a follow-up call the next day. Cost to Ritz-Carlton: maybe $150-300. Outcome: a guest who tells everyone they know about the recovery rather than the original problem.
setup_requirements: SERVQUAL questionnaire template
GAP model diagram handout
Case study: Ritz-Carlton service standards or Disney service training program
Service recovery role-play scenarios

review.value: Marketing services with the playbook designed for goods produces frustration and failure. The four service characteristics demand different marketing tactics: tangibilizing the intangible, training employees as part of the product, managing variability through standards, and balancing capacity with demand

In service businesses, the employees ARE the product. A brilliant external campaign cannot survive contact with poorly trained, demoralized, or under-empowered employees. The Service Profit Chain shows that internal marketing (employee training, motivation, empowerment) is the foundation of external service quality

Services will fail; that is unavoidable. The brands that win in services are the ones that recover so well from failures that customers become more loyal afterward. Empower employees to fix problems on the spot, train them in recovery scripts, and treat recovery as an investment rather than a cost

Service quality breaks down at five points: not understanding what customers expect, not setting standards that match expectations, not delivering to standards, not communicating accurately about what is delivered, and the resulting gap between customer expectation and perception. Diagnose which gap is open and fix it directly

class_schedule.topic: Marketing Metrics and ROI: CAC, LTV, ROAS, and Attribution
learning_objectives: Calculate Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC), Customer Lifetime Value (LTV), and the LTV:CAC ratio for a real or hypothetical business
Apply ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) and Marketing Mix Modeling to evaluate campaign and channel performance
Distinguish single-touch (last-click, first-click) from multi-touch attribution models and explain their limitations
Recognize common marketing metric pitfalls (vanity metrics, attribution distortion, survivorship bias) and how to avoid them
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 5 - Creating Long-Term Loyalty Relationships and Ch. 22 - Managing the Total Marketing Effort
Paul Farris et al., Marketing Metrics: The Definitive Guide to Measuring Marketing Performance (3rd ed.), Ch. 1-3, 5, 10
Avinash Kaushik, Web Analytics 2.0 (2010), Ch. 1-4
Harvard Business Review, 'Are You Undervaluing Your Customers?' (Fader, Hardie & Lee, 2020) on customer-based corporate valuation

lecture_notes.introduction: If you cannot measure marketing's contribution to the business, you cannot defend its budget or improve its performance. Marketing has historically been the most poorly measured function in the company, partly because of the complexity of attributing customer behavior to marketing actions and partly because marketers have sometimes preferred not to be measured. That excuse is gone. Modern digital channels produce torrents of data, and even traditional channels can be measured through advanced techniques like Marketing Mix Modeling and incrementality experiments. The CFO no longer accepts 'brand-building takes time' as an excuse for not measuring; modern CMOs are expected to defend every dollar of marketing spend with data. This session covers the core metrics that matter and the analytical frameworks that make them meaningful. CAC and LTV are the foundation: how much does it cost to acquire a customer, and how much profit does that customer produce over their lifetime? The ratio between them is the single best indicator of whether a marketing strategy is working. ROAS measures the immediate return on a specific ad spend. Attribution distributes credit for conversions across the multiple touchpoints that influenced them. Marketing Mix Modeling estimates the incremental impact of each channel using statistical regression on historical data. Each of these tools has strengths and weaknesses, and the marketer who understands all of them can give the CFO defensible answers. The marketer who relies on a single metric or vanity metrics like impressions and likes is exposed and replaceable.
key_concepts: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) = total marketing and sales spend in a period divided by new customers acquired in that period; should include paid media, content production, salaries, agency fees, tools, and any other acquisition-related costs

Customer Lifetime Value (LTV) = average order value x purchase frequency x gross margin x average customer lifespan; or in subscription businesses: ARPU x gross margin / churn rate; should be discounted to present value for long-lifetime customers

LTV:CAC ratio: the rule of thumb is that healthy businesses have LTV at least 3x CAC; below 3:1 indicates over-spending on acquisition or under-monetizing customers; above 5:1 may indicate under-investment in growth (could be growing faster)

Payback period: months until cumulative gross profit from a customer equals the CAC to acquire them; SaaS benchmarks suggest payback under 12 months indicates efficient growth, over 24 months indicates capital risk

ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) = revenue generated by ad spend / ad spend; useful for tactical campaign evaluation but ignores margins and cannibalization; ROAS of 3.0 means $3 of revenue per $1 spent, but at 30 percent gross margin that is only $0.90 of gross profit

Attribution models: last-click (100 percent credit to final touchpoint, default in many systems but biased toward bottom-funnel channels), first-click (100 percent to first touchpoint, biased toward top-funnel), linear (equal credit across all touchpoints), time-decay (more credit to recent touchpoints), position-based (more to first and last), data-driven (machine learning based on actual conversion paths)

Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM): statistical regression analyzing historical sales data with marketing spend as inputs and other variables (seasonality, weather, price, competitor activity) as controls; produces estimates of incremental contribution by channel; the gold standard for cross-channel measurement, increasingly important post-cookie
Incrementality testing: holding out a control group from advertising exposure to measure true incremental effect, distinguishing real impact from customers who would have converted anyway
Vanity metrics: impressions, likes, follows, page views, raw click counts; easy to measure and hard to connect to revenue; useful as leading indicators but never as primary KPIs

Brand health metrics: aided and unaided awareness, brand consideration, purchase intent, Net Promoter Score (NPS), brand equity scores; lagging indicators of long-term brand-building investment that pure performance marketing metrics will miss
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculate the unit economics for a hypothetical D2C subscription mattress brand. Average order value (mattress + accessories): $1,200. Repeat purchase rate: 25 percent of customers buy again within 5 years (replacement, gifts). Average customer lifespan: 5 years. Gross margin: 55 percent (high for a physical product because direct distribution removes retail markup). Refund rate: 12 percent (mattresses with sleep trials have high return rates). Net AOV after refunds: $1,056. Net gross margin per customer (first purchase): $1,056 x 0.55 = $581. Repeat purchase contribution: 25 percent x $581 = $145. Total LTV: $581 + $145 = $726. Now CAC: marketing spend last quarter was $1.2M, new customers acquired were 1,500. CAC = $800. LTV:CAC ratio = 726 / 800 = 0.9. The business is losing money on every customer; the unit economics are broken. What can be done? Option 1: reduce CAC by improving conversion rates, shifting budget to lower-cost channels (organic, content), or relaxing growth targets. Option 2: increase LTV by raising prices, adding accessories (pillows, sheets, frames), launching second mattress lines for repeat customers, or building a referral program. Option 3: change the model entirely (e.g., financing options that increase order size, or B2B sales to hotels that have different unit economics). Many D2C mattress brands (Casper, Purple) hit exactly this problem and either restructured or went bankrupt. The lesson: LTV:CAC is the metric that determines whether a business model works.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet template with CAC, LTV, payback period calculators
Sample Google Analytics 4 attribution report
MMM case study (e.g., Procter & Gamble or Anheuser-Busch published cases)
Marketing Metrics textbook (Farris et al.)

review.value: If your LTV:CAC ratio is below 3, you are over-spending on acquisition or under-monetizing customers. Below 1, every customer makes the business worse off. This is the single most important metric for any acquisition-focused business model and should be tracked monthly at the executive level

No attribution model captures the full truth. Last-click overstates bottom-funnel channels; first-click overstates top-funnel; multi-touch and data-driven get closer but still miss offline touchpoints. Use multiple methods and triangulate, do not bet the budget on a single attribution view

The only way to know if marketing actually caused a sale is to compare exposed vs unexposed groups. Holdout testing, geo experiments, and ghost ads (running creative in some markets but not others) produce honest measures of incremental impact that no attribution model can match

Impressions, likes, follows, and page views feel like progress but rarely correlate with revenue. Build measurement frameworks that connect marketing activity to business outcomes (revenue, gross profit, customer count, retention). Anything that cannot be tied back to dollars is decoration

class_schedule.topic: Marketing Ethics, Sustainability, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks (utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics) to common marketing dilemmas including targeting, pricing, advertising claims, and data privacy
Distinguish authentic sustainability marketing from greenwashing and identify the FTC Green Guides standards for environmental claims
Evaluate the role of stakeholder capitalism, ESG, and societal marketing in contemporary brand strategy
Synthesize the course content into an integrated framework connecting strategy, customer understanding, marketing mix, and measurement
readings: Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (16th ed.), Ch. 23 - Tapping into Global Markets and Ch. 22 - Managing the Total Marketing Effort
Philip Kotler and Nancy Lee, Corporate Social Responsibility: Doing the Most Good for Your Company and Your Cause (2005)
Federal Trade Commission, 'Green Guides: Guides for the Use of Environmental Marketing Claims' (16 CFR Part 260)
Harvard Business Review, 'The Truth About CSR' (Rangan, Chase, Karim, 2015)

lecture_notes.introduction: Marketing has the power to shape what people buy, what they aspire to, and how they understand themselves. With that power comes ethical responsibility that the discipline has not always honored. Tobacco marketing knowingly addicted millions and lied about it for decades. Pharmaceutical marketing of OxyContin in the late 1990s and 2000s helped fuel an opioid epidemic that killed hundreds of thousands of Americans. Sub-prime mortgage marketing pushed loans on borrowers who could not afford them and helped trigger the 2008 financial crisis. Volkswagen's clean diesel marketing was built on rigged emissions tests. Each of these was profitable in the short term and devastating in the long term, both for the people harmed and for the firms responsible. Marketing ethics is not just about following the law (though that is the floor); it is about distinguishing between what you can do and what you should do. The strongest brands today (Patagonia, Ben & Jerry's, REI, Method) have built their identities around ethical and sustainable practices, and the research is clear that younger consumers (Gen Z especially) increasingly choose brands whose values align with their own. But this creates the temptation of greenwashing: claiming sustainability credentials that are not backed by real practice. The FTC Green Guides exist precisely to police misleading environmental claims, and consumers and journalists have become sophisticated at calling out brands that overclaim. This final session covers the ethical dimensions of marketing decisions, the real (vs performative) practice of sustainability marketing, and synthesizes the course into an integrated framework you can carry into your career.
key_concepts: Three ethical frameworks for marketing decisions: utilitarianism (maximize total well-being, the rule for most cost-benefit analysis), deontology (some actions are wrong regardless of outcome, e.g., deceiving customers, violating privacy), virtue ethics (act as a person of good character would act, the test of 'would you be proud if this appeared on the front page of the newspaper?')

Common marketing ethical dilemmas: targeting vulnerable populations (children, elderly, low-income), exaggerated advertising claims (puffery vs deception), surveillance-based targeting and privacy, pricing of essentials (insulin, EpiPens), planned obsolescence, dark patterns in checkout flows, manipulation of reviews and testimonials

FTC regulation of marketing: Section 5 of the FTC Act prohibits 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices'; specific guidelines cover endorsements (16 CFR 255), green marketing (16 CFR 260), made in USA claims, native advertising disclosure, and influencer disclosure (#ad)

Children and marketing: COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act) restricts data collection from children under 13; the Children's Advertising Review Unit (CARU) enforces self-regulation of ads to children

Privacy regulation: GDPR (EU 2018), CCPA/CPRA (California 2020/2023), Virginia, Colorado, and other state laws; principles include consent, data minimization, purpose limitation, right to access, right to delete, breach notification

Greenwashing: making environmental claims that are unsubstantiated, exaggerated, or misleading; the FTC Green Guides require that claims be specific, substantiated, and not overstate the environmental attribute

Authentic sustainability marketing: requires genuine operational changes (not just marketing claims), measurable goals, transparent reporting, third-party verification (B Corp, Fair Trade, FSC, LEED), and willingness to acknowledge gaps and progress

Stakeholder capitalism: the view (articulated in the 2019 Business Roundtable statement) that companies should serve all stakeholders (customers, employees, suppliers, communities, environment) rather than only shareholders; reshaped corporate communications even where actual practice has been inconsistent

ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance): investor framework for evaluating companies on non-financial criteria; influences capital allocation, executive compensation, and strategic priorities; controversial in 2024 as some investors and politicians have pushed back

Cause-related marketing: tying brand promotion to a social cause (Toms Shoes one-for-one model, Patagonia's 1 percent for the planet, Dawn dish soap's wildlife rescue partnership); effective when authentic and sustained, ineffective or harmful when seen as opportunistic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a contrast between authentic sustainability marketing and greenwashing. Authentic example: Patagonia's 'Don't Buy This Jacket' Black Friday 2011 campaign in The New York Times. The full-page ad showed a Patagonia jacket and asked readers not to buy it unless they truly needed it, citing the environmental impact of producing it. This worked because it was backed by real operational practices: Patagonia's Worn Wear program repairs old gear for free, Patagonia uses recycled materials and tracks supply chain impact, the company donates 1 percent of revenue to environmental causes, and founder Yvon Chouinard transferred ownership of the company to a trust dedicated to fighting climate change in 2022. Result: revenue grew over the following decade because customers rewarded the authenticity. Contrast with greenwashing example: Volkswagen's 'Clean Diesel' marketing campaign (2009-2015) claimed VW diesel cars were environmentally friendly while the company secretly installed defeat devices that detected emissions tests and reduced pollution only during testing, emitting up to 40 times the legal NOx limit in real driving. The fraud was exposed in 2015 by EPA testing. VW paid over $30 billion in fines and settlements, criminal charges were filed against executives, and the brand still has not fully recovered. Then synthesize the course: marketing creates value when it identifies real customer needs, designs offerings that genuinely deliver value, prices fairly, distributes accessibly, and communicates honestly. Marketing destroys value (and eventually the firm) when any of these breaks down. The ethical and the strategic are not opposites; they converge over time. End the course by asking each student to articulate the marketing concept in their own words and apply it to a brand they admire and a brand they do not.
setup_requirements: FTC Green Guides PDF
Patagonia case studies (HBR or company website)
Volkswagen Diesel case study materials
Course-wide synthesis framework handout

review.value: Marketing ethics and marketing strategy converge over the long run. Brands that deceive customers, exploit vulnerable populations, or harm the environment may profit in the short term but eventually pay through regulation, lost trust, and brand collapse. The strongest long-term marketing strategies are also ethical strategies

Modern consumers and journalists are skilled at detecting unsubstantiated environmental claims. The reputational damage from being exposed as a greenwasher exceeds any short-term gain from making the claim in the first place. If you cannot back up an environmental claim with operational reality and third-party verification, do not make it

Across all the frameworks and tactics in this course, one principle holds: customer-focused organizations that create more value than competitors, and capture fair value in return, are the ones that thrive. Every other marketing tool is in service of this principle. Forget the principle and the tools become instruments of waste or harm

Marketing is not a department; it is a discipline that should inform every customer-facing decision in any organization. Whether your career goes into marketing specifically, general management, entrepreneurship, or another field, the customer orientation, strategic thinking, and analytical rigor you have built in this course will serve you for decades

quizzes.topic: Marketing Concept, Environment, and Consumer Behavior

questions.question: Blockbuster's CEO famously turned down an offer to buy Netflix for $50 million in 2000, reportedly laughing the offer out of the room. By 2010 Blockbuster filed for bankruptcy and Netflix had a market capitalization of approximately $9 billion (now hundreds of billions). Using the marketing-myopia concept and the customer-value equation, explain why Blockbuster failed and what their leadership should have understood instead.
questions.answer:
Blockbuster's failure is the textbook example of marketing myopia (Levitt 1960) - defining the business by what it sells (rented video tapes from physical stores) rather than the customer need it serves (convenient, affordable home entertainment).

What leadership should have understood:

1. Customer need analysis: customers were not 'hiring' Blockbuster for the experience of driving to a store, browsing aisles, paying late fees, and returning physical tapes. They were hiring it for affordable home entertainment. Anything that delivered home entertainment more conveniently would eventually win the customer.

2. Customer value equation breakdown of the Blockbuster experience:
- Perceived benefits: access to selection of movies, browsing experience
- Perceived costs: monetary ($4-5 per rental), time (drive to store, browse, drive home, return trip), psychological (late fees were a constant irritant; Blockbuster collected $800 million in late fees in 2000)

3. Value equation breakdown of the Netflix DVD-by-mail offering:
- Same benefits: access to a far larger selection (Netflix could stock long-tail titles a physical store could not)
- Lower costs: no driving, no late fees, predictable subscription pricing, mailbox delivery

4. The strategic question Blockbuster should have asked: 'If our customer need is convenient home entertainment, what is the most convenient way to deliver it, and is anyone working on a more convenient solution?' The answer was DVDs by mail in 2000 and streaming by 2007. Blockbuster could have built either, but they defined themselves as a store rather than as a service.

5. Compounding errors: late fees were a major revenue source (~$800M/year), so Blockbuster's leadership was institutionally hostile to any model that eliminated them, even though customers hated late fees and would defect to anyone who offered an alternative. The very thing that produced near-term profit was the wedge that competitors used against them.

What the leadership should have done: define the business as 'in-home entertainment' rather than 'video rental stores,' actively explore digital distribution as a transformative threat rather than a fringe opportunity, accept short-term profit cannibalization from new models to defend long-term position, and recognize that Netflix's $50M offer was a chance to buy the future cheap.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies marketing myopia and product-vs-need distinction
Applies customer value equation comparing Blockbuster vs Netflix benefits and costs
Explains the strategic implications including the late-fee profit trap

questions.question: You are a marketing analyst for a regional grocery chain operating 80 stores in the Midwest. The CEO asks you to conduct a PESTEL analysis specifically focused on identifying threats to the business in the next 5 years. Walk through each PESTEL category, identify at least one specific force per category, and identify which 2 forces deserve the most strategic attention. Justify your prioritization.
questions.answer:
PESTEL analysis for a regional Midwest grocery chain:

Political:
- Possible changes to SNAP (food stamp) eligibility and benefit amounts (significant for stores in lower-income areas where SNAP can be 10-25 percent of revenue)
- Trade policy and tariffs affecting imported produce and processed foods
- Antitrust posture toward grocery consolidation (Kroger-Albertsons merger blocked in 2024)

Economic:
- Persistent food inflation affecting consumer behavior and shifting share to discount formats (Aldi, Walmart, Costco)
- Wage pressure from tight labor markets and minimum wage increases
- Recession risk affecting discretionary purchases (premium products, prepared meals) more than staples

Social:
- Aging Midwest population (growing senior segment with different shopping patterns - smaller baskets, more frequent trips, prefer in-store)
- Generational shift: Gen Z and Millennials prefer convenience, prepared foods, online ordering, sustainability claims
- Continued shift toward private label as trust in store brands grows
- Health consciousness driving organic, plant-based, and clean-label demand

Technological:
- E-commerce and online grocery growth (Instacart, Amazon Fresh, Walmart pickup) - accelerated by COVID and persisting at 12-15 percent of grocery sales
- AI-powered personalization and dynamic pricing
- Self-checkout and cashierless store technology (Amazon Go)
- Supply chain visibility and shrinkage prevention

Environmental:
- Climate impacts on agriculture causing supply chain volatility (e.g., 2022 California drought affecting produce prices)
- Plastic packaging regulations and consumer demand for sustainable packaging
- Food waste regulations and the cost/PR opportunity of donation programs

Legal:
- Wage and hour laws, scheduling laws (predictive scheduling in some states)
- Food safety regulations (FSMA), product liability
- Privacy law affecting loyalty program data use (CCPA-style laws spreading)

Two forces deserving the most strategic attention:

1. The combination of food inflation and the rise of discount formats (Economic + Social): this is the most immediate existential threat. Aldi and Walmart are taking share precisely from regional mid-tier chains during inflationary periods because cost-conscious shoppers trade down. The chain must respond with aggressive private label expansion, sharper everyday pricing on key value items (KVIs), and either differentiate up-market with quality/experience (e.g., Wegmans model) or compete head-on with the discounters on price.

2. E-commerce and online grocery (Technological): this represents 12-15 percent of category sales now and growing. A regional chain that does not have a credible online ordering and pickup/delivery offering loses the share that shifts online entirely. Building this capability requires picking technology partners (Instacart vs proprietary), training staff for picking, and rethinking store layout. The cost is high but the alternative (gradual share loss) is worse.

The other forces matter but are lower-priority because they unfold more slowly or affect competitors symmetrically.

rubric.key: Identifies at least one specific force in each of the 6 PESTEL categories
Selects 2 forces with clear justification of why they are top priority
Demonstrates strategic thinking about how the forces should change the chain's behavior

questions.question: A subscription meal-kit company has noticed a problem: customers who sign up via a 50 percent off promotion churn at 65 percent within 3 months, while customers who sign up at full price churn at only 30 percent in 3 months. The marketing director wants to expand the 50 percent off promotion to drive growth. Using consumer behavior and behavioral economics concepts, explain why the discount-acquired customers are churning at much higher rates and recommend a better strategy.
questions.answer:
Why discount-acquired customers churn at 2x the rate of full-price customers:

1. Self-selection bias on price sensitivity: customers who respond to a 50 percent off promotion are by definition price-sensitive shoppers who chose the brand because of the discount, not because of intrinsic appeal. When the discount expires, they have no remaining reason to stay.

2. Anchoring effect: the first price a customer pays becomes their reference point. A customer who paid $40 for the first box anchors their perceived value at $40; when the regular price of $80 appears, it feels like a 100 percent price increase, triggering loss aversion and cancellation.

3. Cognitive dissonance and commitment: customers who paid full price had to justify a higher purchase to themselves, creating commitment and consistency (Cialdini). They mentally invested in the decision being a good one. Discount customers had no such commitment because the entry cost was low.

4. Job-to-be-Done mismatch: full-price customers were 'hiring' meal kits for genuine needs (cooking variety, time saving, learning to cook). Discount customers were 'hiring' the offer for the deal itself, which is not a sustainable need.

5. Habit formation failure: research on habit loops shows that habits form through repeated cue-routine-reward cycles. Three months may be insufficient to form a meal-kit cooking habit, especially for users who never strongly intended to adopt the behavior.

6. Reference group effects: discount-acquired customers often signed up because they saw an ad targeting price-sensitive deal hunters (Slickdeals, RetailMeNot, deal-focused Facebook groups). Their reference group culture is to chase the next deal, not to stick with one brand.

Why expanding the promotion is a bad strategy:

- Math: if discount customers churn at 65 percent, only 35 percent become recurring customers; LTV is collapsed by both higher churn AND lower future revenue per customer (they may demand future discounts to stay).
- Brand damage: customers who paid full price see neighbors paying half-price for the same product, creating resentment and reducing willingness to pay full price in the future.
- Margin erosion: 50 percent discount on a 30 percent gross margin product means selling at -20 percent gross margin (a loss on every box).

Better strategy recommendations:

1. Replace deep discount with smaller percentage off and high commitment: e.g., 25 percent off first month with a 3-month commitment, reducing both the price-sensitive self-selection and the anchoring effect.

2. Test 'free upgrade' rather than discount: e.g., free dessert add-on, free premium ingredient, or extra meal for the first month. This reframes the perceived value without anchoring on a low price.

3. Improve the onboarding experience to drive habit formation: cooking tutorials, easy first recipes, post-meal feedback collection, customer success outreach in weeks 2-4.

4. Segment promotions: limit deep discounts to channels and audiences that show high LTV regardless of acquisition discount (e.g., referrals from existing customers).

5. Focus on referral programs: customers acquired via referral from existing customers retain better than discount-acquired because they come with social validation and matched expectations.

6. Build retention: loyalty program, anniversary credits, surprise upgrades for long-tenured customers - retention is cheaper than acquisition and produces better unit economics.

rubric.key: Identifies at least 4 distinct consumer-behavior reasons for the churn gap
Explains why expanding the discount makes the problem worse
Provides at least 3 specific alternative strategies grounded in consumer behavior concepts

questions.question: A startup is developing a new electric scooter for urban commuters. They have $80,000 for marketing research before deciding whether to launch. Design a phased research plan that uses the right methods at each stage. Specify the method, sample size, key questions answered, approximate cost, and how each phase informs the next.
questions.answer:
Phased research plan for the electric scooter:

Phase 1 - Secondary research and exploratory qualitative ($10,000, 3 weeks):

Method: secondary research (industry reports from Statista, IBISWorld on micromobility) plus 15 in-depth interviews with potential urban commuters ages 22-45 in 3 target cities.
Sample: convenience sample recruited via screener survey to ensure target demographic.
Key questions: What is the current state of urban commuting for this segment? What pain points exist with existing options (subway, bike, e-scooter rentals like Lime/Bird)? What would they want in an owned electric scooter vs renting? What are their concerns (theft, charging, weather, regulation)?
Output: hypothesized customer needs, vocabulary they use to describe the problem, initial concept reactions.

Phase 2 - Quantitative descriptive survey ($25,000, 4 weeks):

Method: online survey via panel provider (e.g., Qualtrics, Cint) with 1,200 respondents, urban commuters in 6 target cities, ages 22-45, household income $50K+.
Sample size: 1,200 allows 5 percent margin of error overall and 10 percent in subgroups of 200.
Key questions: market size estimation (how many in target meet criteria), interest in concept, willingness to pay at three price points ($499, $799, $1,199), feature importance ratings, current commuting methods and dissatisfaction, brand preferences in adjacent categories.
Output: market sizing, segment-level demand estimates, optimal price range.

Phase 3 - Causal/conjoint testing ($30,000, 5 weeks):

Method: choice-based conjoint analysis with 800 respondents from the target segment, testing 5 attributes: price ($499/799/999/1,199), range (15/25/35/45 miles), weight (15/25/35 lbs), folding mechanism (yes/no), warranty (1/2/3 years).
Sample: 800 respondents balanced across the same 6 cities.
Key questions: which attributes drive choice the most, what tradeoffs customers are willing to make, what optimal feature bundle exists for each customer segment, what share of preference each scooter configuration would capture vs competitor scooters.
Output: optimal product configuration and pricing, market share forecast.

Phase 4 - Real product test ($15,000, 6 weeks):

Method: test 50 prototype units with 50 households in 2 cities for 4 weeks, with weekly diary entries and end-of-test interviews.
Sample: recruited from Phase 2 respondents who indicated high purchase interest.
Key questions: actual usage patterns (how often, how far, what conditions), durability and reliability issues, customer service issues, satisfaction, willingness to recommend (NPS), perceived value vs price.
Output: validated product-market fit and identification of any critical flaws to fix before launch.

Total cost: $80,000. Total time: approximately 18 weeks.

Rationale for the phasing: each phase builds on the previous one. Phase 1 keeps later phases from asking the wrong questions. Phase 2 sizes the opportunity and identifies the right segments. Phase 3 optimizes the product offering and price. Phase 4 catches any deal-breakers before launch. Skipping any phase would create risk: skipping Phase 1 means surveys ask the wrong questions; skipping Phase 4 means launching a flawed product at scale.

rubric.key: Four distinct research phases with appropriate methods (qualitative, descriptive quantitative, causal, in-use)
Realistic sample sizes and budget allocation summing to ~$80K
Clear logic for how each phase informs the next, with risk explanation

questions.question: A brewery has launched a new craft IPA and wants to position it in a competitive market dominated by Sierra Nevada, Stone, Lagunitas, and Sam Adams. Apply the segmentation, targeting, and positioning (STP) framework to this scenario. Identify a target segment, write a positioning statement using the standard template, and explain how it differs from the listed competitors.
questions.answer:
STP for a new craft IPA:

Segmentation: the craft beer market can be segmented by:
- Beer style preference (IPA, lager, sour, stout)
- Drinking occasion (after-work, weekend social, special occasion, with food)
- Sophistication (curious newcomer, regular craft drinker, beer geek/enthusiast)
- Lifestyle/values (local-focused, environmental, community-oriented, social media-driven)
- Demographic (Millennials and Gen X dominate craft, with growing Gen Z entry)

Target segment: Millennial and Gen Z craft beer drinkers (ages 25-40) in mid-sized urban markets who value local, environmentally responsible breweries and discover new beers via Untappd, Instagram, and word of mouth from beer-knowledgeable friends. This segment is large enough to support a regional brewery, accessible through digital and trade channels, distinct from mass-market beer drinkers, and currently underserved by the listed competitors who are now national brands and have lost their 'local' authenticity.

Frame of reference: craft IPAs in the $11-13 per six-pack range, distributed regionally rather than nationally.

Point of difference: this brewery is hyperlocal (sources hops and grain from within 100 miles, brews exclusively for a 4-state region, never sells outside the region), commits 1 percent of revenue to local watershed conservation, and runs taproom events that build community.

Reason to believe: visible sourcing partnerships with local farms (named on the can), B Corp certification, annual public sustainability report, partnership with local environmental organizations.

Positioning statement (using template):

'For Millennial and Gen Z craft beer drinkers in [region] who value local sourcing, environmental responsibility, and brewery community, [Brewery Name] IPA is a hyperlocal craft IPA that sources every ingredient from within 100 miles and gives 1 percent of revenue to local watershed conservation. Unlike Sierra Nevada, Stone, Lagunitas, or Sam Adams, [Brewery Name] is brewed exclusively for our region and never shipped outside it, making every can a celebration of our place.'

How this differs from competitors:

- Sierra Nevada: founded 1980 in Chico, CA, was once the local craft pioneer but is now a national brand distributed in all 50 states; its size has eroded its 'craft' authenticity.
- Stone: founded 1996 in California, also national; known for aggressive hop-forward IPAs and irreverent branding.
- Lagunitas: now owned by Heineken (acquired 2017); positioning has shifted toward mainstream as it scales.
- Sam Adams: technically not craft (Boston Beer Co. is too large under Brewers Association rules); often perceived as 'mainstream craft' rather than authentic.

The new brewery's positioning explicitly attacks the loss of authenticity and locality that comes with national scale. By committing to never distribute outside the region, the brewery turns its limitation into a feature: scarcity, exclusivity, and authenticity. This is a strategy of intentional smallness that scales would force the larger competitors to abandon. The 1 percent for the planet commitment provides a credible reason to believe that resonates with the values of the target segment.

Marketing implications: the brewery should never try to expand nationally (it would invalidate the position); should partner heavily with local restaurants, farmers markets, and music venues; should invest in Instagram and Untappd content showing the local sourcing; should host community events that reinforce locality.

rubric.key: Clear segmentation with multiple bases and rationale for chosen target
Positioning statement following the standard template with all four elements
Specific contrast against named competitors showing the differentiation

questions.question: Stitch Fix uses extensive customer data and personal stylists to send personalized clothing boxes. Use the buying center concept (typically applied to B2B) creatively to analyze a household clothing purchase decision. Then identify three distinct consumer segments Stitch Fix could target and the different marketing approach each segment would need.
questions.answer:
Applying the buying center concept to household clothing purchases:

The traditional buying center applies to organizations, but household decisions also involve multiple roles that marketers can analyze:

- User: the person who will wear the clothing (e.g., the husband whose wife is sending him a Stitch Fix box because he hates shopping)
- Influencer: family members, friends, social media references, fashion influencers
- Decider: who actually decides to subscribe (often the spouse who wants the user to dress better)
- Approver: the household budget owner who approves the spend (often the same as the decider)
- Buyer: the person who handles the transaction (creates the account, enters payment, manages returns)
- Gatekeeper: the person who controls access to the user (e.g., a wife who wants her husband dressed better might be both decider and gatekeeper, ordering the box without her husband's input)

This matters for Stitch Fix marketing: the user (the man receiving the box) and the buyer/decider (the wife placing the order) may be different people with different motivations. Marketing that targets only the user misses the buyer; marketing that targets only the buyer misses the user's needs.

Three distinct Stitch Fix target segments and marketing approaches:

1. The Time-Strapped Professional Woman (ages 32-50, full-time job, family, no time to shop):

- Need: wants to dress well for work and life but has no time or energy to shop
- Job-to-be-done: 'help me look professional and put-together without having to think about it'
- Marketing approach: emphasize convenience and time savings; testimonials from women like her; focus on professional wardrobe building; partnerships with working-mom influencers; price as 'less than your monthly Starbucks habit'
- Channel: Facebook and Instagram targeting working women with kids; partnerships with productivity and parenting brands
- Creative: real customer stories about getting time back

2. The Style-Anxious Man (ages 28-45, professional, knows he should dress better but does not know how):

- Need: wants to look better but is intimidated by clothing stores and does not know what works
- Job-to-be-done: 'tell me what to wear so I do not have to figure it out'
- Marketing approach: emphasize the personal stylist (a real human picking clothes for him), reassurance that nothing he hates will be sent, easy returns; use 'dressed by a stylist' framing; before/after testimonials; focus on workplace appropriate styles
- Channel: LinkedIn ads, men's lifestyle sites (Esquire, GQ digital), podcasts like Tim Ferriss; YouTube ads on tech and gaming channels
- Creative: relatable stories about confidence gained from dressing better

3. The Plus-Size Customer Underserved by Traditional Retail (ages 25-55, sizes 14-24+):

- Need: cannot find stylish clothing in their size at most physical stores; online shopping is hit-or-miss for fit
- Job-to-be-done: 'find clothes that fit me well and look good without the embarrassment of in-store shopping'
- Marketing approach: emphasize the inclusive sizing range, fit guarantee, body-positive imagery, stylists trained in plus-size styling; lead with images of women who look like the customer; offer free returns and easy exchanges; partner with body-positive influencers
- Channel: Instagram with size-inclusive influencers; partnerships with plus-size fashion bloggers; targeted Facebook ads in body-positive communities
- Creative: customer stories specifically from plus-size customers showing the brand's commitment

Each segment has different needs, channels, creative requirements, and even pricing sensitivities. Stitch Fix's underlying service is the same, but the marketing must be segment-specific to win each one.

rubric.key: Creative application of buying center to household decisions
Three distinct consumer segments with clear differentiation
Specific marketing approach for each segment including channels and creative

questions.question: A direct-to-consumer mattress brand is debating its pricing strategy. The current mattress costs $400 to manufacture and is sold for $899. A competitor has launched a similar mattress at $699. The CEO suggests cutting the price to $749 to be competitive. The CMO argues for keeping the price at $899 and adding value instead. Using value-based pricing concepts and analyzing both options, recommend a strategy with clear reasoning.
questions.answer:
Analysis of the two options:

Current state:
- Cost: $400
- Price: $899
- Gross margin per unit: $499 (55.5 percent)
- Competitor at $699

Option A: cut price to $749
- Margin per unit drops to $349 (46.6 percent)
- Margin reduction: $150 per unit, or 30 percent margin loss per unit
- For this to be profitable, unit volume must increase by at least 43 percent ($499/$349 - 1 = 0.43) just to maintain total gross profit
- Risk: starts a price war; the competitor may cut further, eroding margins on both sides
- Brand impact: signals that the original $899 was 'inflated' or that the brand was overpriced; trains customers to wait for discounts; anchoring damages future pricing power

Option B: maintain price and add value
- Maintains $499 margin per unit
- Adds value through: extended trial period (120 days vs current 100), free white-glove delivery and old mattress haul-away (cost: $50/order), free pillow and sheet set with purchase (cost: $40), 15-year warranty vs current 10-year (cost: ~$10 per unit in expected warranty claims)
- Total added cost: ~$100 per unit
- New effective margin: $399 (44.4 percent) - similar to Option A's margin but at full price
- Brand impact: reinforces premium positioning; differentiation moves from price to total customer experience; competitor cannot easily match the augmented offering without similar cost

Recommendation: Option B (maintain price, add value) is the better choice for these reasons:

1. Avoids a price war: matching the competitor's $699 gives them the option to cut to $599, dragging the entire category down. Mattress D2C had a documented race to the bottom in 2018-2020 that put several brands out of business (Casper had to restructure, Tuft & Needle exited public markets via acquisition).

2. Preserves brand equity: the mattress is positioned as a quality product. Cutting price creates a perception that the original price was inflated, eroding brand equity for both new and existing customers.

3. Augmented product approach: per the three levels of product, value is created at the augmented level (delivery, trial, warranty, accessories) more easily than at the actual product level. Customers have shown willingness to pay for these augmentations.

4. Targets the right customer: customers who buy mattresses primarily on price will defect to the cheaper competitor regardless of how much the price is cut. Customers who buy on quality, brand, and risk reduction (long trial, warranty) will stay with the higher-priced option if the value justifies it.

5. Math is similar: both options reduce per-unit margin by approximately the same amount ($150 vs $100), so the financial impact is comparable, but Option B preserves brand equity and pricing power for the future.

Caveats and risks:

- Option B requires that customers actually perceive the added value (white-glove delivery is a real benefit; sheet sets are easy to undervalue)
- The brand must invest in marketing communications to make the augmented offer visible (not just bury it in the fine print)
- If volume drops significantly because customers cannot perceive the value differential, the brand may need to revisit
- Worth running a controlled test in 1-2 markets before committing to either option

Alternative consideration: introduce a new lower-tier product at $699 to compete directly with the competitor while keeping the flagship at $899. This is line stretching downward, used by Toyota with Scion and BMW with the 1-Series. Risks include cannibalization and brand dilution.

rubric.key: Quantitative analysis of both options with margin impact calculated
Application of value-based pricing principles and brand equity reasoning
Clear recommendation with specific risks and possible alternatives acknowledged

questions.question: Apple charges $159 for AirPods (2nd gen), $249 for AirPods (3rd gen), and $549 for AirPods Max. Use product line pricing concepts and the 'good-better-best' framework to explain Apple's strategy. Why would Apple offer three tiers instead of just one?
questions.answer:
Apple's three-tier AirPods pricing demonstrates several product line pricing strategies working together:

1. Good-Better-Best framework:

- Good: AirPods 2nd gen at $159 - the entry point, basic features, charging case (no wireless charging on standard model)
- Better: AirPods 3rd gen at $249 - improved sound, water resistance, longer battery, spatial audio, wireless charging case
- Best: AirPods Max at $549 - over-ear premium headphones, active noise cancellation, premium materials (aluminum, leather, mesh), best sound quality

2. Price laddering rationale: by offering multiple price tiers, Apple captures customers across the willingness-to-pay curve rather than forcing a binary choice. A customer who would pay $159 but not $249 still becomes a customer (and an iPhone ecosystem reinforcement) instead of going to a competitor or going without.

3. Anchoring and decoy effects:

- The $549 AirPods Max anchors the price ladder at the high end, making the $249 mid-tier feel reasonable by comparison
- The 3rd gen at $249 is the volume seller because it sits between the basic and premium tiers; behavioral research shows the middle option captures the largest share when three are presented (the 'compromise effect')
- Without the AirPods Max, the 3rd gen would feel expensive next to the 2nd gen; the existence of the $549 option shifts perception

4. Captive customer strategy: Apple's broader strategy is to keep customers in the Apple ecosystem. AirPods are a low-cost (relative to iPhone) entry into the ecosystem that creates lock-in via the H1/H2 chip integration with iPhones, Mac, iPad, and Apple Watch. Even the cheapest AirPods at $159 reinforce the ecosystem and increase customer LTV.

5. Margin economics: Apple's margins on accessories like AirPods are estimated at 60-70 percent gross margin, much higher than iPhone margins. The premium tiers (3rd gen and Max) likely have even higher margins. Apple is willing to maintain a low-margin entry point to attract price-sensitive customers because they create lifetime value through ecosystem reinforcement.

6. Why three tiers instead of one:

- Maximizes total revenue across the customer base: more price points = more customers served
- Captures price discrimination opportunity: customers willing to pay $549 should pay $549; customers only willing to pay $159 should not be lost
- Creates upgrade paths: customers who buy the 2nd gen may upgrade to the 3rd gen or Max in the future
- Provides differentiation against competitors: cheaper bluetooth earbuds compete with the $159 tier; high-end headphones (Bose, Sony) compete with the $549 tier; without three tiers Apple would lose to competitors at one of the ends
- Supports gift purchases: $159 is a gift price point, $249 is a higher-end gift, $549 is a luxury gift; multiple tiers serve multiple gift occasions

7. Strategic risk: cannibalization. Customers who would have bought the $249 might buy the $159 instead, depressing average selling price. Apple manages this by ensuring meaningful feature differences between tiers - the 3rd gen has better sound, water resistance, and spatial audio that justify the upgrade for customers who value those features.

8. Lessons for marketers: when designing product lines, ensure that (a) each tier has meaningful differentiation that justifies the price gap, (b) the spacing between tiers anchors customers toward the desired tier (often the middle), (c) the high-end anchor is psychologically valuable even if it sells in low volume, and (d) the low-end tier is profitable enough that even price-sensitive customers contribute to profit and ecosystem.

rubric.key: Identification of good-better-best framework with specific feature differentiation
Explanation of anchoring, decoy, and compromise effects in pricing
Strategic reasoning including ecosystem effects, captive customer, and cannibalization tradeoffs

questions.question: Patagonia is known for its strong brand built on environmental commitment. In 2011, they ran a Black Friday ad in The New York Times titled 'Don't Buy This Jacket.' Sales actually increased significantly that year. Using brand equity concepts (Aaker and Keller), explain why this counterintuitive ad worked. What would have happened if a different brand (e.g., Nike or H&M) had run the same ad?
questions.answer:
Why the 'Don't Buy This Jacket' ad worked for Patagonia:

1. Alignment with brand identity: Patagonia had spent decades building its brand around environmental responsibility and against consumerism. The 'Don't Buy This Jacket' message was not a contradiction; it was the most concentrated possible expression of what the brand had always stood for. Customers who knew Patagonia immediately recognized the message as authentic.

2. Aaker brand equity dimensions:

- Brand awareness: the ad generated massive media coverage (NYT, CNN, BBC, hundreds of articles), increasing awareness beyond what the ad budget alone could achieve
- Perceived quality: by telling customers not to buy the jacket and instead repair their existing gear, Patagonia signaled extreme confidence that its products were worth keeping for years (low quality products would not be worth repairing)
- Brand associations: reinforced existing associations of environmental commitment, anti-consumerism, durability, authenticity, founder values (Yvon Chouinard's well-known environmental advocacy)
- Brand loyalty: existing customers became more loyal because their purchase decisions were validated; the brand they chose was the brand that asks them to buy less

3. Keller CBBE pyramid analysis:

- Salience: the ad dramatically increased mental availability ('which brand cares about the environment?' - Patagonia comes to mind)
- Performance: reinforced the durability claim implicit in 'repair instead of replace'
- Imagery: deepened the imagery of the brand as principled, courageous, willing to sacrifice short-term sales for long-term values
- Judgments: customers judged Patagonia as credible, trustworthy, superior on the dimensions they cared about
- Feelings: warmth (we are not just selling things), self-respect (buying Patagonia is a choice that aligns with my values), social approval (visible Patagonia gear signals values to others)
- Resonance: customers became advocates, telling friends about the ad, posting on social media, identifying as Patagonia people

4. Why sales increased despite the message: the message attracted new customers who shared the values, deepened loyalty among existing customers, and created earned media that no paid budget could match. The 'don't buy' message was paradoxically the most effective sales message because it differentiated Patagonia from every competitor selling Black Friday discounts.

What would happen if Nike or H&M ran the same ad?

Nike:
- Brand identity is built on athletic achievement, performance, and aspiration ('Just Do It')
- Asking people not to buy would feel inconsistent with the brand and perceived as performative
- Customers and journalists would point to Nike's labor practices, supply chain emissions, marketing budget, and shareholder pressure as evidence that the message is hypocritical
- Likely outcome: backlash, accusations of greenwashing, no sales lift, possibly sales decline
- Why it would fail: there is no operational or historical foundation for the message; the gap between identity and image would widen rather than narrow

H&M:
- Brand identity is built on fast fashion: cheap, trendy, disposable
- Asking people not to buy would directly contradict the entire business model
- H&M has been repeatedly accused of greenwashing (its 'Conscious Collection' was investigated and judged misleading)
- The fast fashion industry is the second-largest polluter globally and is responsible for textile waste at scale
- Likely outcome: severe backlash, viral mockery, accusations of cynical marketing, sales decline
- Why it would fail: the message would be obviously inauthentic and would highlight the gap between H&M's claims and its operations

Key lesson on brand equity: the same message can be powerful or destructive depending on whether it aligns with the brand's accumulated identity and operational practice. Patagonia could run 'Don't Buy This Jacket' because they had spent decades earning the right to say it through real practices (Worn Wear repair program, 1 percent for the planet, B Corp certification, recycled materials, transferring company ownership to a climate trust). Brands that have not earned that right cannot borrow the message; they will be exposed and damaged by the attempt.

This is the difference between brand equity built through years of consistent action and a campaign that tries to claim values the brand has not lived. Brand equity is real and measurable; it is also the result of decades of choices that other brands cannot replicate quickly.

rubric.key: Application of Aaker and/or Keller brand equity frameworks
Specific contrast with at least one other brand showing why the message would fail
Strategic insight about authenticity, operational backing, and brand-building over time

quizzes.topic: Channels, IMC, and Digital Marketing Foundations

questions.question: Nike announced in 2017 a strategic shift to direct-to-consumer (DTC), reducing wholesale partners from over 30,000 to a focused list of approximately 40 strategic accounts and pushing customers toward Nike.com, the Nike app, SNKRS, and Nike-owned stores. By 2024, Nike was reversing course and rebuilding wholesale relationships. Diagnose what went right and wrong with the strategy using channel marketing concepts.
questions.answer:
What went right initially (2017-2021):

1. Brand control: by reducing wholesale, Nike could control how its product was presented, priced, and merchandised. Wholesale doors had inconsistent execution, off-brand surroundings, and frequent discounting that damaged premium positioning.

2. Customer data ownership: every DTC sale captured email, purchase history, and behavioral data, enabling personalization, retargeting, and lifetime value extraction that wholesale could not provide.

3. Margin capture: bypassing wholesale removed the retailer markup, theoretically capturing 30-50 percentage points more margin per unit (though offset by DTC fulfillment, marketing, and customer service costs).

4. Channel conflict reduction: by limiting wholesale distribution, Nike reduced inter-retailer price wars and the discounting that eroded brand equity.

5. Initial financial results: 2020-2021 DTC growth was strong, partly aided by COVID-19 lockdowns shifting consumer behavior to online channels. Nike's stock peaked in late 2021 with the strategy seemingly validated.

What went wrong (2022-2024):

1. Lost shelf space went to competitors: when Nike pulled out of stores like Foot Locker, Dick's Sporting Goods, DSW, and others, those stores filled the space with competitors.
